Algorithm and Protocol for Preparing Healthy Individuals for PSTF Lasting More than 24 h and up to 72 h\n4. A Graphical Summary of the Effects of PSTF Lasting More than 24 h and up to 72 h\n5. General Discussion and Future Directions\n6. Methodology\n- Cross-sectional, case–control, and randomized control trials, and retrospective, prospective cohort or cohort epidemiological studies were included.\n- Only studies conducted in humans were included.\n- Only full-text articles in English were included.\n- Non-human experimental models were excluded.\n- Non-English papers were excluded because of the structural language barrier.\n- Articles that were not peer-reviewed were excluded.\n- Studies involving fasting periods for religious reasons or those exceeding 72 h were also omitted.\n- Studies classified as meta-analyses or systematic reviews were excluded.\n7. .\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:5093:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v1\PG_LB_FS_01_health.json:385:      "content": "A Comprehensive Perspective on the Biological Effects of Intermittent Fasting and Periodic Short-Term Fasting: A Promising Strategy for Optimizing Metabolic Health\nHighlights\n- Fasting exerts multidimensional effects, including triggering metabolic switching and improving immune function, redox balance, the adipokine profile, and mTOR pathway regulation, among others.\n- The modulation of the mTOR pathway through fasting shows promise, but there is a lack of consensus regarding its implementation; context-dependent regulation is crucial to avoid adverse effects and ensure safe clinical implementation.\n- Despite its documented metabolic benefits, prolonged short-term fasting (PSTF) remains under-researched and may be associated with mild, self-limiting symptoms that require appropriate monitoring.\n- A novel algorithm and protocol were developed to prepare healthy individuals for PSTF that lasted more than 24 hours and up to 72 hours.\n- Combining fasting with individualized physical activity is essential to preserve skeletal muscle mass and enhance overall metabolic outcomes.\nAbstract\n1. Introduction\n2. Impact of Various Fasting Regimens on Metabolic and Cellular Signaling Pathways\n2.1. Overview of Different Intermittent Fasting Patterns\n2.2. Effect of Various TRE Schemes on Metabolic and Signaling Pathways\n2.3. Effect of Various PSTF Schemes on Metabolic and Signaling Pathways\n| Fasting Time Frame | Duration | Study Design | Study Population | Positive Metabolic Outcomes | Negative Metabolic Outcomes | First Author/[Ref.] |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 24 h | 10 weeks | Single-center, randomized controlled trial | 88 females overweight females | ↓ glucose, ↓ insulin, ↓ HOMA-IR, ↑ BHB | ↓ insulin sensitivity, ↑ AST | Hutchison et al., 2019 [58] |\n| 24 h | Single protocol | Randomized controlled crossover trial | 30 healthy cases (20 women and 10 men) | ↑ GH, the observed changes in GH levels occurred independently of any associated weight loss | Not reported | Horne et al., 2025 [60] |\n| 24 h | 26 weeks | Randomized controlled trial | 38 adults | ↓ HOMA-IR, ↓ insulin, ↓ fasting glucose, ↓ metabolic syndrome score, ↓ diastolic blood pressure, ↑ HDL-C | Not reported | Bartholomew et al., 2021 [61] |\n| 24 h | 26 weeks | Randomized controlled trial | 68 adults | ↓ HOMA-IR, ↓ systolic blood pressure, ↓ diastolic blood pressure, ↓ HR, ↓ fat: lean ratios, improved lipide profile | ↓ T3 | Stekovic et al., 2019 [63] |\n| 24 h | 8 weeks | Randomized parallel-arm trial | 8 healthy adults (4 male and 4 female) | ↓ body weight, ↓ BMI, ↓ FFM | ↓ SMM | Herz et al., 2024 [51] |\n| 36 h | Single protocol | Randomized crossover study | 20 healthy adults (11 male and 9 female) in Il-1, Il-6, TNF-α,IL-8, hs-CR, adiponectin levels | van Herpen et al., 2013 [71] |\n| 72 h | Single protocol | FAST*BRAIN study | 15 healthy females | ↓ body weight, ↓ glucose, ↑ ketone bodies | ↑ BDI-2 score | Ding et al., 2018 [59] |\n| 72 h | Two analyses were conducted | Randomized crossover study | 8 healthy males | ↓ insulin, ↓ C-peptide, ↓ glucose, ↓ mTOR, ↑ FFA, ↑ glucagon, ↑ blood flow | ↓ free and total T3 | Vendelbo et al., 2014 [72] |\n| 72 h | Single protocol | Controlled metabolic study (lean vs. obese, pre–post design) | 18 healthy men, subjects (9 normal-weight and 9 obese) | ↓ insulin, ↓ C-peptide, ↓ glucose, ↑ FFA, ↑ glucagon,↔ in cortisol levels, ↓ mTOR, ↑ p62 | Not reported | Bak et al., 2016 [73] |\n3. Algorithm and Protocol for Preparing Healthy Individuals for PSTF Lasting More than 24 h and up to 72 h\n4. A Graphical Summary of the Effects of PSTF Lasting More than 24 h and up to 72 h\n5. General Discussion and Future Directions\n6. Methodology\n- Cross-sectional, case–control, and randomized control trials, and retrospective, prospective cohort or cohort epidemiological studies were included.\n- Only studies conducted in humans were included.\n- Only full-text articles in English were included.\n- Non-human experimental models were excluded.\n- Non-English papers were excluded because of the structural language barrier.\n- Articles that were not peer-reviewed were excluded.\n- Studies involving fasting periods for religious reasons or those exceeding 72 h were also omitted.\n- Studies classified as meta-analyses or systematic reviews were excluded.\n7. .\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:5096:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:250:      "content": "Abstract\nObjectives To evaluate the effect of different artificial aging methods on the bond strength of a resin composite associated with a universal adhesive (Scotchbond Universal) used under two etching approaches (self-etch [SE] or etch-and-rinse [ER]) to enamel and dentin substrates.\nMaterials and Methods A total of 96 noncarious human third molars were prepared and randomly divided according to three factors ( n = 6): substrate (enamel and dentin), adhesive approach (SE and ER), and aging method (water storage for 24 hours, 6 months, or 1 year; subjected to 10,000, 20,000, or 30,000 thermal cycles; and sodium hypochlorite [NaOCl] storage for 1 or 5 hours).\nStatistical Analysis Microshear bond strength tests were conducted, and the collected data (MPa) were subjected to three-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with post hoc Bonferroni tests ( p < 0.05) and Weibull analysis. The failure pattern was also evaluated.\nResults Three-way ANOVA revealed that the factors “substrate” ( p = 0.00) and “aging method” ( p = 0.00) had a significant effect on the bond strength, but the factor “adhesive approach” did not ( p = 0.84). The bond strength in the enamel group for the SE approach was negatively affected under 20,000 and 30,000 thermal cycles. Weibull presented the highest m in the NaOCl storage for the 5 hours group to enamel using the SE and to dentin using ER approaches. Adhesive/mixed failures were predominant for all groups.\nConclusion Thermocycling aging (20,000 and 30,000 cycles) significantly reduced the bond strength to enamel using the SE approach. On the contrary, storage with the NaOCl method proved to increase bond strength under the evaluated conditions.\nKeywords: artificial aging, interface degradation, hydrolysis, universal adhesive\nIntroduction\nHuman enamel and dentin tissues present clear differences regarding their micromorphology and the balance of organic and inorganic contents, which consequently result in different performance of adhesive systems onto such tissues. 1 2 The interface between the resin composite and the cavity walls is the most critical region in bonded restorations. Unprotected collagen depleted fibrils 3 and chemical hydrolysis of the ester bonds are considered the main reasons for hybrid layer degradation. These processes occur concomitantly and definitively contribute to reducing bond strength over time. 4\nThe newest universal adhesives enable dentists to apply the adhesive following a self-etch (SE), etch-and-rinse (ER), or an alternative selective enamel etching approach. The latter is an association of an ER approach onto enamel and the SE approach to dentin. 5 Universal adhesives in the literature are described as a single-bottle and no-mix adhesive system which performs satisfactorily using any adhesion strategy and to any substrate considering the restorative dentistry scenario (dental tissues, as well as different direct or indirect restorative materials). 6\nIn vitro aging methods have been considered a must in any laboratorial adhesion study, as they may help the researchers better understand and predict the performance of adhesives systems concerning bonding degradation. 7 Thus, microshear bond strength testing (μSBS) values may represent valuable information concerning the influence of different aging methods on bond strength. Different aging methods such as storage in water, 4 thermocycling, 8 and storage in sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) solution, 9 as well as associations between them 10 have been applied to predict the clinical behavior and its repercussions on the bonding efficacy in the long term of the adhesive systems. 11 12 Despite these studies, comparisons and guidance to which is the best in vitro aging method for these objectives are lacking.\nAging by storage in water commonly uses immersion of the samples in distilled water at 37°C from 3 to 12 months 13 to partially simulate the oral environment conditions. Moreover, 10,000 to 30,000 cycles 8 are used in the thermocycling method, in which hot and cold water intermittently can stimulate the hydrolysis of unprotected collagen fibrils by repetitive contraction/expansion stress, resulting in gap propagation along the adhesive interface, thereby allowing water and pathogenic oral fluid penetration. 14 15 The storage method in 10% of NaOCl solution from 1 to 5 hours 10 15 uses the nonspecific proteolytic effect properties of the hypochlorite to degrade the organic resin and tooth interface components (as unprotected collagen fibrils) to simulate the aging effect. 9 10 15\nThus, based on the limited information in the current literature comparing distinct aging methods and its effect on enamel and dentin, the present study aimed to evaluate and compare the effect of different artificial aging methods on the bond strength of composite resin restorations associated with a universal adhesive under μSBS. The null hypothesis is there would not be differences regarding the different aging methods in promoting interfacial bond degradation.\nMaterials and Methods\nStudy Design\nThis in vitro study was approved by the local ethics committee in research (protocol number 2.054.447, CAAE 67687417.9.0000.5346). A total of 96 noncarious and crack-free human third molars were collected from an institutional teeth bank up to 3 months after extraction by clinical indications. The teeth were stored in 0.5% chloramine-T at 37°C during 7 days to disinfection, since it has no influence on bond strength. 16\nSpecimen Preparation\nThe roots were cut off and the crowns were mesiodistally sectioned using a low-speed water cooled diamond saw in a cutting machine (Labcut 1010, ExtecInc, Enfield, Connecticut, United States). The sections were embedded in PVC rings with self-curing acrylic resin (JET, Classico Art Od, São Paulo, Brazil). The thickness of smear layer was standardized by using a 600-grit silicon carbide paper for 1 minute in a circular mechanical polishing machine (EcoMet 250; Buehler, Lake Bluff, Illinois, United States) under water irrigation. 17\nApproved samples were then randomized and divided into 32 groups ( n = 6) according to three factors: “adhesive approach” (2): ER and SE; “substrate” (2): enamel and dentin; and “aging methods” (8): water storage at 37°C for 24 hours (W24h as control group), 6 (W6m), 12 months (W12m); thermocycling in 10,000 (T10,000), 20,000 (T20,000), or 30,000 (T30,000) cycles; and 10% NaOCl storage for 1 (SH1h) and 5 hours (SH5h) as described in Table 1 .\nTable 1. Experimental design.\n| Study factors | |||||||||\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| Adhesive approach | Substrate | Aging method | |||||||\n| W24h | W6 m | W12 m | T10,000 | T20,000 | T30,000 | SH1 h | SH5 h | ||\n| Etch-and-rinse | Dentin | Samples prepared and immediately stored in distilled water at 37°C for 24 h, 6 mo, or 12 mo before testing For the longer periods (6 and 12 mo), water was changed weekly |\nSamples thermocycled 10,000; 20,000; or 30,000 times at 5–55°C for 30 s each batch, 5 s of transfer time, before testing | Samples kept immersed in 10% NaOCl solution at room temperature for 1 or 5 h, before testing | |||||\n| Enamel | |||||||||\n| Self-etch | Dentin | ||||||||\n| Enamel |\nBonding and Restoration Procedures\nA universal adhesive (Scotchbond Universal—3M ESPE Dental Products; St. Paul, Minnesota, United States) using SE and ER approaches was applied according to the manufacturer’s instructions to enamel and dentin ( Table 2 ). Next, three starch tubes (Renata; PastificioSelmi, Londrina, Parana, Brazil) of 1 mm height and 0.96 mm internal diameter hole were positioned on mesial, central, and distal thirds over each substrate before adhesive photocuring, as is described by Tedesco et al (2013). 18 A light-emitting diode (Emitter D—Shuster Eq. Od. Ltd, Santa Maria, Brazil) with 900 mW/cm 2 light output was used to light activate the adhesive for 10 seconds, and then, a resin composite (Filtek Z350 XT shade A2; 3M ESPE Dental Products) was used to fill in the starch tubes, followed by light activation using the same light unit for 20 seconds.\nTable 2. Materials used in this study—application procedure.\n| Material | Manufacturer | Composition | Usage approach | |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| SE approach | Etch-and-rinse approach | |||\n| Abbreviations: 2-HEMA, 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate; 10-MDP, 10-methacryloyloxydecyl dihydrogen phosphate; Bis-EMA, bisphenol A polyethylene glycol dimethacrylate; Bis-GMA, bisphenol A glycidyl dimethacrylate; SE, self-etch; TEGDMA, triethylene glycol dimethacrylate; UDMA, urethane dimethacrylate. | ||||\n| 37% phosphoric acid | Condac 37—FGM, Joinville, SC, Brazil | 37% phosphoric acid, filler, pigment and deionized water | – | 1. Apply phosphoric acid gel for 20 s 2. Rinse with water 3. Dry with cotton pellets |\n| Adhesive system | Scotchbond Universal—3M ESPE Dental Products, St. Paul, Minnesota, United States | 2-HEMA, 10-MDP, phosphate monomer, dimethacrylate resins, methacrylate-modified, polyalkenoic acid copolymer, filler, ethanol, water, initiators, silane | 1. Actively apply the adhesive to the prepared tooth for 20 s 2. Gently air-dry for 5 s 3. Light activated for 10s |\n1. Steps 1 to 3 of the above cell 2. Apply adhesives as described in the SE approach 3. Light activated for 10 s |\n| Resin composite | Filtek Z350—3M ESPE Dental Products | Silica 20 nm, zirconia 4–11 nm, Bis-GMA, Bis-EMA, UDMA, TEGDMA | 1. Apply two increments of resin composite 2. Light activated for 20 s after each increment |\nFinally, the samples were stored for 24 hours in distilled water at 37°C, and the starch matrix was removed with air–water spray. The enamel/resin and dentin/resin interfaces were examined using a stereomicroscope at ×10 magnification (Discovery V20, Zeiss; Oberkochen, Germany), and specimens which presented interfacial defects such as bubbles or voids were substituted.\nMicroshear Bond Strength Test\nAfter the aging process, the μSBS tests were executed using the wire loop technique 19 with a 0.2-mm stainless steel wire attached to a specific device in a universal testing machine (EMIC DL 1000; Instron, S.J. Pinhais, Brazil). Shear load was applied at a crosshead speed of 1.0 mm/min with cell of 1 kN until failure of the bonded restoration. Caution was taken for the parallelism between the wire, the load cell, and the adhesive-resin interface, as well as to make the load application as close as possible to the bond interface.\nFailure Analysis\nThe failed specimens were examined by a trained and blinded single operator in a stereomicroscope (Discovery V20, Zeiss) at ×40 magnification to determine the failure mode, which was categorized as adhesive/mixed, cohesive in enamel/dentin, or cohesive in resin composite.\nStatistical Analysis\nAll statistical tests were performed at OriginPro 2015 (OriginLab Co.; Northampton, Massachusetts, United States) with a significance level of 5%. The bond strength result in MPa was considered as the measuring unit for each sample. Thus, after assuring parametric data distribution (Shapiro–Wilk’s test), the three-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used for the factors “adhesive approach,” “substrate,” and “aging method.” Multiple comparisons were tested with post hoc Bonferroni. In addition, a Weibull statistical analysis using the maximum likelihood method was run to obtain the shape (Weibull modulus— m ) parameter for each condition. 20 21 The Weibull modulus is a measure of the variability of the results, 22 where a low value of m indicates a wide scatter of results, while a high value of m indicates a close grouping of fracture stress values. Thus, the value of m can be interpreted as giving an indication of the dependability of the material. 23\nStatistical differences of Weibull parameters were detected by maximum likelihood estimation (absence of overlap of 95% confidence intervals).\nResults\nThe μSBS expressed in MPa and standard deviations are summarized in Table 3 . Three-way ANOVA for repeated measures revealed that the factors “substrate” ( p = 0.00) and “aging method” ( p = 0.00) had significant effects on bond strength. Meanwhile, the factor “adhesive approach” (SE or ER) did not ( p = 0.84). The triple interaction (adhesive substrate aging method) also did not present a statistically significant effect ( p = 0.86). The “in pairs” interactions revealed a significant effect on the comparisons of “substrate” × “aging method” ( p = 0.01), and the “adhesive approach” × “aging method” ( p = 0.00).\nTable 3. Microshear bond strength, standard deviation, and statistical significance.\n| Adhesive approach | Substrate | W24 h | W6 m | W12 m | T10,000 | T20,000 | T30,000 | SH1 h | SH5 h |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| Abbreviations: D, dentin; E, enamel; ER, etch-and-rinse; SE, self-etch; SH1h, NaOCl storage for 1 hour; SH5h, NaOCl storage for 5 hours; T10,000, thermocycling in 10,000; T20,000, thermocycling in 20,000; T30,000, thermocycling in 30,000; W24h, water storage at 37°C for 24 hours; W6m, water storage at 37°C for 6 months; W12m, storage at 37°C for 12 months. Notes: Different lowercase letter in lines indicate statistical significant differences by three-way analysis of variance and Bonferroni post hoc tests (α= 0.05). As the “adhesive approach” factor proved not to be statistically significant, Bonferroni post hoc tests for such factor were omitted. | |||||||||\n| SE | E | 12.02 (3.85) ab | 12.06 (3.52) ab | 10.32 (5.23) ab | 9.70 (3.73) bc | 7.00 (3.74) c | 8.26 (3.38) c | 12.56 (3.95) ab | 13.15 (2.82) a |\n| D | 15.59 (3.55) b | 23.66 (5.89) a | 18.51 (7.70) ab | 20.26 (5.55) ab | 14.97 (6.45) b | 17.40 (6.18) ab | 20.97 (6.99) ab | 25.66 (6.18) a | |\n| ER | E | 15.52 (3.34) ab | 11.49 (5.52) b | 13.80 (5.82) ab | 15.63 (4.03) ab | 17.73 (4.55) a | 13.72 (6.91) ab | 17.13 (5.19) a | 17.66 (5.17) a |\n| D | 13.42 (4.22) bc | 7.52 (7.63) c | 13.20 (8.33) bc | 16.34 (5.97) ab | 15.92 (9.77) ab | 11.16 (7.87) bc | 21.16 (6.75) ab | 22.79 (4.59) a |\nThe results showed that the different aging methods promoted distinct results for each approach of adhesive and substrates. The bond strength in the enamel group using the SE approach under 20,000 (7.00 MPa) and 30,000 (8.26 MPa) thermal cycles was negatively affected in comparison with the control group (W24h) (12.02 MPa). Six months of water storage using the SE adhesive approach for both substrates affected the bond strength less using 20,000 thermal cycles. The opposite happened when the ER approach was used, in which water storage for 6 months affected the bond strength more than the 20,000 thermal cycles and storage in hypochlorite regardless of the time.\nTable 4 summarizes the Weibull distribution presenting the highest m in the SH5h group for ER (5.42) to dentin, while the highest m to enamel was presented for the W24h group (5.48), also using the ER approach. The probability plot regarding failure over time for enamel and dentin is presented separately for each aging method ( Fig. 1A C ). It was noticed that the NaOCl aging presented more predictable results in both substrates, since the confidence interval was higher than the other aging methods.\nTable 4. Weibull analysis—modulus, confidence intervals, and statistical significance.\n| Adhesive approach | Substrate | W24 h | W6 m | W12 m | T10,000 | T20,000 | T30,000 | SH1 h | SH5 h |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| Abbreviations: D, dentin; E, enamel; ER, etch-and-rinse; SE, self-etch; SH1h, NaOCl storage for 1 hour; SH5h, NaOCl storage for 5 hours; T10,000, thermocycling in 10,000; T20,000, thermocycling in 20,000; T30,000, thermocycling in 30,000; W24h, water storage at 37°C for 24 hours; W6m, water storage at 37°C for 6 months; W12m, storage at 37°C for 12 months. Note: Different lowercase letter in lines indicate statistical significant differences depicted by maximum likelihood estimation via Weibull analysis (absence of overlap of 95% confidence intervals). | |||||||||\n| SE | E | 3.80 (2.58–5.61) ab | 3.96 (2.77–5.68) ab | 2.25 (1.55–3.26) a | 4.63 (3.13–6.86) ab | 3.11 (2.14–4.52) ab | 3.47 (2.42–4.91) ab | 3.45 (2.46–4.85) ab | 5.32 (3.76–7.51) b |\n| D | 4.51 (3.24–6.28) a | 4.45 (3.15–6.28) a | 3.58 (2.47–5.17) a | 4.05 (2.86–5.73) a | 3.14 (2.15–4.59) a | 3.21 (2.23–4.63) a | 3.33 (2.25–4.91) a | 4.68 (3.29–6.64) a | |\n| ER | E | 5.48 (3.79–7.93) b | 2.29 (1.62–3.25) a | 2.70 (1.85–3.94) ab | 4.77 (3.28–6.92) b | 4.17 (2.98–5.82) ab | 3.64 (2.51–5.28) ab | 3.82 (2.65–5.51) ab | 4.20 (2.85–6.17) ab |\n| D | 3.70 (2.56–5.33) ab | 1.94 (1.27–2.74) a | 3.08 (2.12–4.46) ab | 3.06 (2.13–4.39) ab | 1.84 (1.25–2.71) a | 2.44 (1.71–3.47) a | 3.76 (2.57–5.51) ab | 5.42 (3.83–7.68) b |\nAdhesive/mixed failures were predominant for all groups, regardless of the substrate, adhesive, or aging methods ( Table 5 ).\nTable 5. Failure mode.\n| Groups | Failure mode (%) | |||\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| Adhesive/mixed | Cohesive in enamel | Cohesive in dentin | Cohesive in resin | |\n| Control (W24 h) | 100% | – | – | – |\n| W | 80% | 5% | 15% | – |\n| T | 86% | 4% | 10% | – |\n| SH | 100% | – | – | – |\nDiscussion\nThis study focused on testing and comparing the effects of different aging methods on the bond strength of a universal adhesive using SE and ER approaches to dentin and enamel substrates. In vitro studies of bond stability associated with aging methods may be considered more reliable to predict adhesive performance. Despite this, it is suggested that the mechanism of bond degradation observed in laboratory techniques may not be applied at the same rate clinically and the bond strength has a secondary role in the clinical success of bonded restorations. 24\nThe null hypothesis was rejected, since the different aging methods studied affected the bond strength independently of the adhesive approach and substrate.\nWater Storage\nThe most frequent aging method related in the literature is the storage in distilled water under room temperature or 37°C, since this method has been generally accepted as a way to predict the time in which an adhesive interface would remain stable. 1 12 13 Studies performed with water storage indicate that degradation was accelerated by hydrolysis of the hydrophilic resin components and by host-derived proteases with collagenolytic activity. 4\nStorage for 6 months using the ER approach reduced the bond strength more than storage in hypochlorite and 20,000 thermal cycles, suggesting that this method is more effective when using this approach. This also leads us to believe that the SE approach is more resistant to this aging, since the bond strength in this and other studies with universal adhesives remains stable, even after 1 year of water storage. 13 25\nAccording to Scherrer et al, 11 the behavior of these adhesives bonded to dentin is better evaluated using the Weibull distribution function, predicting the likelihood of failure at specific stress levels in which Weibull m reports the variability of the results. The highest m value was reached in enamel and the lowest was in the dentin group, both with the ER approach after 24 hours.\nRegional variability of dentin tissue and more recently chemical bonding component of dental adhesives was added to bond strength studies. 26 27 It was observed that contemporary adhesives follow the tendency of having the 10-methacryloyldecyl dihydrogen phosphate (MDP) in their formulation as a functional monomer. 28 The obtained results in the W24, W6m, and W12m groups onto the dentin substrate using the SE approach revealed that W6m showed an unexpected increase of bond strength comparing with the baseline (W24h), which is in accordance with Kawazu et al (2020). 8 These results can be explained by the composition of adhesive that contains 10 to 15% water which helps ionize the functional resin monomer. 29 The Weibull analysis presented no relevancy since these groups reached m values of 4.51 (W24h), 4.45 (W6m), and 3.58 (W12m). Therefore, it can be assumed that the cited groups showed a regular distribution of defects.\nThermocycling\nResearch protocols conclude that 500 cycles through water baths at 5 and 55°C, as preconized by ISO TR 11450 30 is very limited to promote effects on bond strength and/or microleakage of the adhesive interface. 15 31 Therefore, the studies arbitrarily determine the number of thermocycles, accepting that 10,000 thermocycles correspond to 1 year of in vivo degradation. 14\nBased on this information, this study performed the equivalent of 3 years of clinical use (T30,000) of Scotchbond Universal in both strategies, which may be considered as a reasonable aging time. Under the SE approach, the values for dentin after thermocycling were generally higher for bond strength than enamel. Nevertheless, corroborating a study by Hariri et al, 31 the aging with thermocycling presented more predictable enamel results than is sustained by the Weibull analysis, since the shape, m , and confidence interval values were adequately distributed. The 20,000 and 30,000 cycles of the enamel group using SE approach presented significantly lower results than the baseline, contradicting other studies, 25 32 which could be explained by the use of other bond strength tests and substrate.\nStorage in 10% NaOCl\nIt is known that for clinical uses, the concentration of NaOCl is lower than 10%. In this study, the 10% NaOCl solution is considered an extreme condition of aging, and it was used according to the previous studies 10 33 34 as a guarantee of the organic component of dentin been degraded. This study showed that the storage in 10% NaOCl solution did not reduce the bond strength for any time tested. Other studies which stated lower bond strength had used the same concentration of the NaOCl solution but different adhesives and bond strength testing. 10 33 34 On the contrary, the m showed higher values in dentin with the ER approach in the SH5h group, indicating lower variability (meaning lower spread) in bond strength, which means a high reliability of the characteristic bond strength and probable absence of critical flaws. 11 This may also have been the influence of incorporating the 10-MDP monomer in the adhesive, increasing the SE capability and higher chemical stability and the presence of polyalkenoic acid copolymer, 35 36 which may explain the better results for bond strength. 37\nThe aging methods focused on this study influenced the bond strength to Scotchbond Universal as the adhesive system. These findings support that bond strength remains stable under some artificial aging methods. However, this is an in vitro study, so further clinical trials are needed to better understand the behavior of bonded restorations using other adhesives.\nConclusion\nThermocycling prove to be the method, which promoted the most intense degradation of bond interface and induced the lowest bond strength (especially at 20,000 or 30,000 cycles). On the contrary, storage with NaOCl method proved to increase the bond strength in the evaluated conditions.\nFunding Statement\nFunding This study was funded by the Brazilian Federal Agency for Coordination of Improvement of Higher Education Personnel— CAPES (Finance Code 001).\nFootnotes\nConflict of Interest None declared.\nReferences\n- 1.De Munck J, Van Landuyt K, Peumans M et al. A critical review of the durability of adhesion to tooth tissue: methods and results. J Dent Res. 2005;84(02):118–132. doi: 10.1177/154405910508400204. Health](https://www.eesi.org/topics/public-health)\n# Issue Brief | The State of PFAS Forever Chemicals in America (2024)\n#### By [Molly Brind’Amour](https://www.eesi.org/authors/molly-brindamour)\nSeptember 6, 2024\n**Table of Contents**\n  * [The Prevalence of PFAS](https://www.eesi.org/papers/view/issue-brief-the-state-of-pfas-forever-chemicals-in-america-2024#1)\n  * [Effects on Humans](https://www.eesi.org/papers/view/issue-brief-the-state-of-pfas-forever-chemicals-in-america-2024#2)\n  * [Federal Regulation](https://www.eesi.org/papers/view/issue-brief-the-state-of-pfas-forever-chemicals-in-america-2024#3)\n  * [Federal Legislation](https://www.eesi.org/papers/view/issue-brief-the-state-of-pfas-forever-chemicals-in-america-2024#4)\n  * [State and Local Laws and Regulation](https://www.eesi.org/papers/view/issue-brief-the-state-of-pfas-forever-chemicals-in-america-2024#5)\n  * [Mitigation and Remediation](https://www.eesi.org/papers/view/issue-brief-the-state-of-pfas-forever-chemicals-in-america-2024#6)\n\n  \n---  \nThe term “perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances” (PFAS) refers to a group of thousands of synthetic chemicals with specific heat-, grease-, and water-resistant [**characteristics**](https://www.dhs.wisconsin.gov/chemical/pfas.htm). These substances are distinguished by their [**chemical structure**](https://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/pfas/docs/PFAS_FamilyTree_EnvHealthPro-508.pdf), which includes a chain of fluorine-bonded carbon atoms. PFAS [**entered**](https://www.epa.gov/pfas/our-current-understanding-human-health-and-environmental-risks-pfas) the homes of everyday Americans in the 1940s, and can be found in at least 200 different [**use categories**](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2020/em/d0em00291g#:~:text=Despite%20being%20non%2Dexhaustive%2C%20our,more%20than%201400%20individual%20PFAS.) and [**applications**](https://www.epa.gov/pfas/our-current-understanding-human-health-and-environmental-risks-pfas), including firefighting foam, food wrappers, cosmetics, clothing, cookware, and household products.\nOften referred to as “forever chemicals,” PFAS possess a [**chemical structure**](https://www.vox.com/2022/8/25/23318667/pfas-forever-chemicals-safety-drinking-water) that makes them extremely difficult to break down. Definitions of PFAS [**vary**](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004222002905), so the exact chemicals included under the umbrella term differ from one organization to another. In addition, categories within the PFAS label, such as “long-chain” and “short-chain” PFAS, have different [**properties**](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7657368/), such as shorter or longer half-lives.\nWide usage over many decades has led to the [**presence**](https://www.epa.gov/pfas/our-current-understanding-human-health-and-environmental-risks-pfas) of PFAS in soil and water, thus leading to the contamination of fertilizer, livestock, and drinking water. Furthermore, studies have found that at least 97% of Americans [**tested**](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4483690/) had PFAS compounds in their body fluids. While the health [**effects**](https://www.epa.gov/pfas/our-current-understanding-human-health-and-environmental-risks-pfas) of PFAS are still being explored, evidence suggests that forever chemicals can cause health issues, including reduced fertility, childhood development problems, and increased cancer"
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:3817:outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_11\findings.md:60:All citation markers in the three released reports resolve to bibliography entries:
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4065:outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_10\findings.md:57:No fabricated citation markers were found in the released report: report markers [1]-[9] all resolve to bibliography entries, and `verification_details.json` shows strict verification dropped unsupported numeric AF-ALERT sentences before release.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4087:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:6329:      "abstract": "Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a common chronic autoimmune disease. Neutrophils release and their extracellular traps (NETs) tend to result in synovial inflammation and cartilage damage. Huangqin Qingre Chubi Capsule (HQC) is an important herbal formulation for RA treatment and has been used for many years. This study applied an interdisciplinary and integrated strategy combining clinical data, integrative bioinformatics, molecular modeling, and both in vitro and in vivo experiments to elucidate the efficacy and potential anti-inflammatory mechanisms of HQC for RA. Retrospective clinical data mining demonstrated that patients with RA treated with HQC exhibited recovery of immuno-inflammatory markers and self-perception. By applying network pharmacological analysis, molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulation, and cellular thermal shift assays, we determined that HQC can directly bind to MAPK14 and target p38 MAPK signaling pathway and NETs formation. Treatment of an adjuvant-induced arthritis rat model combining damp-heat patterns using HQC demonstrated it's effectiveness in reducing the severity of inflammatory arthritis in a dose-dependent manner and downregulated phosphorylation of p38 MAPK and NETs release. In vitro co-culture system mimicking inflammatory microenvironment of RA in vivo revealed that crosstalk between neutrophils (PMNs) and fibroblast-like synoviocytes (FLSs) was manifested by activation of p38 MAPK signaling pathway, release of NETs, and amplification of inflammation, which was blocked by HQC. Mechanistic validation using the p38 MAPK agonist diprovocim demonstrated that HQC inhibited NET formation by modulating the p38 MAPK pathway (mainly by inhibiting its phosphorylation) to exert anti-inflammatory effects. In conclusion, our interdisciplinary and integrated study demonstrated that HQC can target the p38 MAPK signaling pathway to inhibit NET formation and inflammatory response, thereby blocking the crosstalk between PMNs and FLSs to ameliorating RA.",
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4110:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:5329:  "draft_report": "# How does damp heat aging affect the adhesion strength of DVS-PEI encapsulants in flexible electronics?\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis systematic review synthesizes evidence from 19 sources examining how damp heat aging affects encapsulant adhesion strength in flexible electronics, with particular attention to the uncharacterized DVS-PEI material class. Under combined thermal-humid exposure at 95°C, adhesive joint tensile strength decreased by 54.4%, nearly double the 31.3% reduction from moisture absorption alone [6], following Arrhenius kinetics proportional to interfacial moisture concentration [3]. Thermal cycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) produced no statistically significant effect on interfacial mechanics (p > 0.05) [4], confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver. EVA at the glass interface degrades preferentially, reaching levels equivalent to 15+ years of field exposure after 10,000 hours of accelerated aging [13]. Industry data reveal that only 69% of module configurations pass damp heat qualification [16], with 15 manufacturers experiencing failures. Formulation optimization identifies 24% VA content in PEVA [15] and 84–90% gel content in EVA [10] as durability optima, while POE demonstrates superior adhesion stability over EVA [5]. A threshold debonding energy of 160 J/m² is proposed for encapsulant interfaces [10]. The most critical gap identified is the near-complete absence of DVS-PEI-specific damp heat aging data across all dimensions—kinetics, failure modes, formulation sensitivity, and field correlation.\n\n## Encapsulant Materials and Damp Heat Test Standards\n\nInterface degradation and bulk encapsulant integrity do not necessarily track together under environmental stress. Thermal degradation at the encapsulant-substrate interface can reach significant levels even when the bulk encapsulant material itself shows negligible degradation in the absence of UV radiation and elevated humidity [REF:1]. This decoupling between interfacial and bulk failure modes underscores the need for adhesion-specific characterization rather than reliance on bulk polymer metrics alone.\n\nAmong encapsulant systems evaluated under accelerated aging conditions, cross-linking encapsulants such as ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA) and polyolefin elastomer (POE) demonstrate sensitivity to processing parameters. Higher lamination temperature proved beneficial for both peel force and gel content, with improvements observed before and after damp-heat aging [REF:2]. Adhesion between encapsulant and glass was characterized through peel tests, while gel content and yellowness index served as complementary aging indicators [REF:2]. The co-measurement of mechanical adhesion alongside chemical aging markers enables a more complete picture of encapsulant performance under stress [REF:2].\n\nThe observation that lamination temperature influences post-aging adhesion retention [REF:2] while interface degradation can proceed independently of bulk degradation [REF:1] points to a complex failure landscape. Process optimization at the lamination stage may mitigate adhesion loss, yet the interfacial degradation pathway operates through mechanisms not fully captured by bulk indicators. Current evidence is limited to EVA and POE systems; the adhesion behavior of other encapsulant families under equivalent damp-heat protocols remains an open question requiring dedicated investigation.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Thermal degradation at the encapsulant-substrate interface can be significant even when the bulk encapsulant experiences negligible degradation without UV and humidity exposure [REF:1].\n- Higher lamination temperature improves both peel force and gel content for cross-linking encapsulants (EVA, POE) before and after damp-heat aging [REF:2].\n- Peel tests, gel content, and yellowness index together provide a multi-indicator assessment of adhesion and encapsulant aging [REF:2].\n- Evidence is currently limited to EVA and POE; other encapsulant families lack equivalent damp-heat adhesion characterization.\n\n## Adhesion Degradation Kinetics Under Damp Heat\n\nAdhesion energy between cell, encapsulant, and glass layers decreases under both hot-dry and hot-humid accelerated aging conditions. Coupon-level testing after 5000 h of exposure at 90°C under ~1% RH (hot-dry) and 60% RH (hot-humid) demonstrated measurable decreases in adhesion energy Gc [REF:5]. The presence of moisture amplifies the degradation pathway: tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by approximately 31.3% after moisture absorption alone, while combined exposure to high temperature (95°C) and humidity drove losses to 54.4% [REF:6]. These quantitative results converge with kinetic modeling that describes the adhesion degradation rate as proportional to the moisture concentration at the interface, governed by an Arrhenius temperature dependence [REF:3]. Higher temperature increases both the rate of chemical reaction and the diffusion coefficient, promoting greater water penetration and further degradation of adhesive joints [REF:6].\n\nThe mechanistic picture emerging from these data distinguishes between reversible and irreversible damage modes. Interface strength reduction, the appearance of microstructure defects, and hydrolysis of the polymer matrix are all irreversible consequences of damp-heat aging [REF:6]. This irreversibility contrasts with the behavior observed under purely thermal cycling. Thermocycling over 10,000 cycles between 5°C and 50°C had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on the interfacial mechanics and fracture mode of either experimental or commercial adhesives [REF:4]. The divergence between moisture-driven and thermally driven outcomes reinforces the kinetic model's emphasis on moisture concentration as the rate-controlling variable at the interface [REF:3][REF:4].\n\n| Aging Condition | Degradation Observed | Source |\n|---|---|---|\n| Hot-dry, 90°C, ~1% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |\n| Hot-humid, 90°C, 60% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |\n| Moisture absorption only | Tensile strength −31.3% | [REF:6] |\n| Combined 95°C + humidity | Tensile strength −54.4% | [REF:6] |\n| Thermocycling, 10,000 cycles, 5–50°C | No significant effect (p > 0.05) | [REF:4] |\n\nComparative performance across encapsulant chemistries reveals material-dependent degradation rates under damp heat. EVA laminates showed less degradation in peel force after damp heat than POE samples across the tested lamination temperatures [REF:2]. POE laminates, by contrast, revealed improved compressive shear performance after damp-heat aging compared to their unaged state [REF:7]. This apparent divergence—EVA outperforming POE in peel force retention while POE improves in shear—indicates that adhesion degradation kinetics are both material-specific and test-mode dependent [REF:2][REF:7]. Peel strength and shear strength capture different interfacial failure mechanisms, and ranking encapsulants by one metric alone risks mischaracterizing their damp-heat durability.\n\nThe kinetic framework linking degradation rate to interfacial moisture concentration and Arrhenius-type temperature dependence [REF:3] provides a unifying model, yet its applicability across all encapsulant families remains unverified. Current quantitative data concentrate on EVA and POE systems [REF:2][REF:7], with adhesion energy measurements on cell/encapsulant/glass configurations [REF:5] and tensile testing on adhesive joints exposed to combined thermo-humid conditions [REF:6]. The absence of equivalent fracture energy (kJ/m²) measurements for TPO or other polyolefin encapsulants under standardized damp-heat protocols represents a significant evidence gap. Similarly, the irreversible nature of hydrolysis-driven degradation [REF:6] raises durability questions that Arrhenius extrapolation from accelerated tests may not fully capture, particularly when the dominant failure mechanism transitions from interfacial debonding to bulk polymer degradation at longer exposure durations.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by 31.3% under moisture absorption alone and by 54.4% under combined high temperature (95°C) and humidity, confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver [REF:6].\n- Adhesion energy Gc decreased after 5000 h of both hot-dry (90°C, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90°C, 60% RH) aging on cell/encapsulant/glass coupons [REF:5].\n- Thermocycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on interfacial mechanics, isolating moisture as the critical variable [REF:4].\n- EVA laminates retained peel force better than POE under damp heat [REF:2], while POE showed improved compressive shear after aging [REF:7], indicating test-mode-dependent degradation ranking.\n- Interface strength reduction, microstructure defects, and polymer matrix hydrolysis are irreversible effects of damp-heat exposure [REF:6].\n\n## Failure Mode Transitions and Interfacial Mechanisms\n\nSubstrate type and aging method exert statistically significant effects on bond strength, while the choice of adhesive approach does not. Three-way ANOVA demonstrated that substrate (p = 0.00) and aging method (p = 0.00) were significant factors, whereas adhesive approach had no significant influence (p = 0.84) [REF:8]. This finding reframes the failure mode question: degradation outcomes depend more on the interface being stressed and the environmental protocol applied than on the adhesive formulation itself [REF:8]. The resistance of resin-dentin bonds to thermal-aging degradation was material dependent [REF:11], and among the adhesive systems tested, one-step self-etch adhesives obtained the lowest microtensile bond strength (MTBS) results after both aging treatments, with significantly reduced adhesive capacity following thermocycling [REF:11]. Not all adhesive chemistries, however, succumb to aging: the experimental non-methacrylate adhesive EA 5 experienced no changes (p > 0.05) in bonding performance following the entire 1-year duration of biochemical aging [REF:4].\n\n| Factor | p-value | Significance | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Substrate | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |\n| Aging method | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |\n| Adhesive approach | 0.84 | Not significant | [REF:8] |\n\nThe chemical degradation pathway in EVA encapsulants under damp heat proceeds through decomposition into acetic acid, which in turn reacts with perovskite layers and causes yellow PbI2 degradation [REF:12]. This acetic acid release represents a failure mechanism specific to EVA chemistry and distinguishes it from non-acetate-containing encapsulants. At the interfacial level, EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, explaining why this initially stronger interface becomes the preferential site for delamination [REF:14]. The differential degradation across the two EVA interfaces [REF:14] aligns with the ANOVA finding that substrate type drives bond strength outcomes [REF:8], as the glass and cell surfaces present fundamentally different chemical environments for moisture interaction and adhesive breakdown.\n\nMaterial properties beyond chemistry further constrain encapsulant durability. PEVA18 showed inadequate transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of damp-heat aging, discouraging its use as an encapsulant [REF:9]. Elevated stiffness after aging increases the mechanical stress at the interface during thermal cycling, potentially lowering the delamination threshold. This stiffness-mediated pathway operates alongside the chemical degradation routes documented for EVA [REF:12][REF:14] and represents an independent contributor to failure mode transitions in polyethylene-vinyl acetate variants [REF:9].\n\nThe long-term trajectory of adhesion loss provides a bridge between accelerated laboratory aging and field performance. At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion falls to a level generally seen after long-term field exposures of more than 15 years [REF:13]. The EVA manufacturer STR has suggested a minimum of 53 N/cm peel strength after lamination (measured by 180° peel) as sufficient to ensure a 20-year lifetime [REF:10]. These two data points—the 10,000-hour laboratory equivalence to >15 years of field exposure [REF:13] and the 53 N/cm durability threshold [REF:10]—together frame the practical boundary conditions. When accelerated aging drives peel strength below this manufacturer-specified floor, the encapsulant system transitions from a regime of gradual interfacial weakening to one where delamination becomes the dominant failure mode.\n\nSeveral methodological limitations constrain these findings. The statistical independence of adhesive approach from bond strength [REF:8] was demonstrated in a specific substrate-adhesive matrix and may not generalize to all encapsulant-substrate combinations encountered in flexible electronics. The EA 5 result showing zero degradation over one year of biochemical aging [REF:4] applies to a non-methacrylate formulation that differs fundamentally from the EVA and POE systems central to photovoltaic encapsulation. The EVA deacetylation pathway producing acetic acid [REF:12] is chemistry-specific and does not extend to polyolefin or thermoplastic encapsulants. Quantitative fracture energy measurements that would enable direct comparison of failure mode transitions across encapsulant families remain sparse in the available evidence.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Substrate type and aging method significantly affect bond strength (p = 0.00 for both), while adhesive approach does not (p = 0.84), indicating that interfacial conditions dominate over adhesive formulation [REF:8].\n- EVA decomposes into acetic acid under damp heat, reacting with perovskite and causing PbI2 degradation, a failure mechanism specific to acetate-containing encapsulants [REF:12].\n- EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, making the initially stronger glass interface the preferential delamination site [REF:14].\n- At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion reaches levels equivalent to >15 years of field exposure, against a manufacturer-recommended minimum of 53 N/cm for 20-year lifetime [REF:13][REF:10].\n- The experimental non-methacrylate adhesive EA 5 showed no degradation (p > 0.05) over 1 year of biochemical aging, suggesting alternative chemistries may resist aging-induced failure modes [REF:4].\n\n## Formulation and Processing Parameters Affecting Durability\n\nVinyl acetate (VA) content exerts a decisive influence on PEVA encapsulant durability under damp-heat conditions. PEVA films with 18, 24, 33, and 40% VA content were cured at 150°C under vacuum and subjected to damp-heat aging at 85°C and 85% RH per IEC 61215 for 1000 and 2000 h [REF:9][REF:15]. PEVA with higher VA content (33% and 40%) degraded significantly after 2000 hours of damp-heat aging, resulting in significant loss of transmittance and mechanical integrity [REF:15]. Tensile strength, thermal stability, and degree of crosslinking were greater for PEVA containing lower VA content (18% and 24%) and diminished for higher VA (33% and 40%) after damp-heat aging [REF:15]. The pattern is consistent across multiple performance axes: lower VA content preserves both mechanical and thermal properties more effectively under prolonged moisture-heat exposure.\n\n| VA Content | Tensile Strength After DH | Thermal Stability After DH | Crosslinking After DH | Encapsulant Recommendation | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 18% (PEVA18) | Higher | Higher | Higher | Discouraged (stiffness, transmittance) | [REF:15][REF:9] |\n| 24% | Higher | Higher | Higher | Recommended | [REF:9][REF:15] |\n| 33% | Diminished | Diminished | Diminished | Not recommended | [REF:15] |\n| 40% | Diminished | Diminished | Diminished | Not recommended | [REF:15] |\n\nThe lowest VA formulation, however, does not represent the optimal choice despite its superior mechanical retention. PEVA18 (18% VA) showed inadequate percent transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of damp-heat aging, discouraging its use as an encapsulant [REF:15]. This creates a formulation window: while higher VA content (33–40%) compromises durability and lower VA content (18%) compromises optical and mechanical functionality, the intermediate 24% VA formulation balances these competing requirements. Both independent investigations converge on the same recommendation—reducing VA content in PEVA to 24% for better encapsulation response against damp-heat aging [REF:9][REF:15].\n\nCrosslink density, quantified through gel content, provides a complementary parameter governing damp-heat resistance. The optimal gel content range for the EVA encapsulant materials tested was 84–90%, where laminates showed the best combined chemical, optical, and adhesion stability [REF:10]. Samples below 70% gel content showed low chemical and optical stability and weak adhesion [REF:10]. Gel content thus serves as a process-quality indicator: encapsulants laminated below the 70% threshold enter service with insufficient crosslink density to withstand hygrothermal stress, while those within the 84–90% range retain interfacial integrity through the aging protocol.\n\nThe interplay between VA content and crosslinking density defines a two-dimensional formulation space for damp-heat durability. Lower VA content (18–24%) preserves crosslinking capacity after aging [REF:15], and higher gel content (84–90%) ensures adhesion retention [REF:10]. The PEVA18 case illustrates that maximizing one parameter without considering the other leads to suboptimal outcomes: despite superior crosslinking retention at low VA, the 18% formulation fails on transmittance and stiffness criteria [REF:15]. The 24% VA target [REF:9][REF:15] combined with the 84–90% gel content window [REF:10] together define the processing envelope most likely to deliver long-term encapsulant performance.\n\nSeveral gaps limit the generalizability of these formulation guidelines. The VA content studies examined PEVA specifically [REF:9][REF:15], and the gel content optimization was conducted on a particular EVA material [REF:10]; whether the 24% VA and 84–90% gel content optima transfer to other polyolefin or thermoplastic encapsulant families remains uncharacterized. The aging protocol followed IEC 61215 at 85°C/85% RH [REF:9], a standardized but singular condition that does not capture the full range of field exposure profiles, including cyclic humidity or UV co-exposure. Dose-response data between VA content and specific degradation products (such as acetic acid yield) are absent from the available claims, limiting mechanistic interpretation of why the 24–33% VA boundary marks the transition from acceptable to unacceptable degradation.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- PEVA with higher VA content (33% and 40%) degraded significantly after 2000 h of damp-heat aging (85°C/85% RH), with loss of transmittance, tensile strength, thermal stability, and crosslinking [REF:15].\n- PEVA18 (18% VA) retained superior mechanical properties but showed inadequate transmittance and high stiffness after aging, ruling it out as an encapsulant [REF:15].\n- Two independent sources recommend reducing VA content to 24% for optimal damp-heat resistance [REF:9][REF:15].\n- The optimal gel content range for EVA is 84–90%, with samples below 70% showing low chemical/optical stability and weak adhesion [REF:10].\n- The intersection of 24% VA content and 84–90% gel content defines the formulation window most protective against damp-heat degradation [REF:9][REF:10][REF:15].\n\n## Field Reliability and Industry Quality Trends\n\nIndustry-scale reliability testing reveals that damp-heat performance remains a significant quality challenge across the photovoltaic module supply chain. In the 2024 PVEL scorecard, only 69% of bills of materials (BOMs) tested degraded by less than 6% power loss after damp-heat testing [REF:16]. Fifteen manufacturers experienced at least one failure during damp-heat testing in the 2024 reporting period [REF:16]. The involvement of multiple manufacturers in these failure events indicates that the problem is distributed across the supply base rather than concentrated in isolated producers.\n\n| Metric | Value | Source |\n|---|---|---|\n| BOMs with <6% power loss after DH | 69% | [REF:16] |\n| Manufacturers with at least one DH failure | 15 | [REF:16] |\n\nThe temporal dynamics of adhesion degradation under damp-heat conditions follow a non-linear trajectory that complicates durability predictions. Both field exposure and accelerated chamber aging reveal a distinct adhesion plateau that emerges during intermediate exposure periods—after 1 year in the field and after 1,000 hours in a chamber [REF:13]. This plateau behavior means that short-duration accelerated tests may overestimate the rate of long-term degradation by extrapolating from the initial steep decline phase, while the plateau phase itself does not guarantee permanent stabilization at acceptable adhesion levels.\n\nA parallel non-monotonic pattern has been observed in single lap joint strength under damp-heat aging: the strength first decreased and then rose with aging time, a trend attributed to post-curing of the adhesive making it harder [REF:6]. This recovery phenomenon contrasts with the monotonic degradation typically assumed in accelerated aging models and has implications for interpreting short-duration test results. The adhesion plateau observed in field and chamber studies [REF:13] and the post-cure-driven strength recovery in lap joints [REF:6] together indicate that adhesion evolution under damp heat is not a simple monotonic decline—transient recovery or stabilization phases intervene, potentially masking underlying progressive degradation.\n\n| Aging Stage | Adhesion Trend | Mechanism | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Initial (<1 year field / <1000 h chamber) | Declining | Moisture ingress, interfacial degradation | [REF:13] |\n| Intermediate plateau | Stabilized | Plateau formation | [REF:13] |\n| Non-monotonic (lap joints) | Decrease then rise | Post-curing hardening | [REF:6] |\n\nThe 69% pass rate in industry testing [REF:16] must be interpreted against the non-linear adhesion trajectories documented in controlled studies [REF:6][REF:13]. The plateau observed after 1,000 hours of chamber aging [REF:13] aligns with standard qualification test durations (IEC 61215 specifies 1,000 h), raising the possibility that modules passing the 1,000-hour checkpoint benefit from plateau-phase stabilization without being tested through the subsequent degradation phase that may follow at longer exposures. The post-curing recovery in lap joints [REF:6] further complicates pass/fail interpretation: a module that appears to have stabilized may simply be in a transient hardening phase.\n\nThe available evidence base for field reliability assessment carries several methodological limitations. The PVEL scorecard data [REF:16] report power degradation thresholds rather than direct adhesion measurements, requiring inference about the relationship between power loss and encapsulant adhesion failure. The adhesion plateau observation [REF:13] was derived from specific module configurations and aging protocols, and its universality across encapsulant chemistries and substrate combinations has not been established. The non-monotonic strength trend in single lap joints [REF:6] involves a post-curing mechanism that is adhesive-specific and may not apply to all encapsulant systems. Longer-duration accelerated aging studies exceeding the plateau phase, combined with direct adhesion measurements at the module level, would strengthen the link between laboratory findings and field failure prediction.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Only 69% of BOMs tested in the 2024 PVEL scorecard degraded by less than 6% power loss after damp-heat testing, with 15 manufacturers experiencing at least one failure [REF:16].\n- A distinct adhesion plateau emerges after 1 year of field exposure and 1,000 hours of chamber aging, indicating non-linear degradation kinetics [REF:13].\n- Single lap joint strength follows a non-monotonic trajectory under damp-heat aging—first decreasing, then rising due to post-curing of the adhesive [REF:6].\n- The coincidence of the adhesion plateau with standard 1,000-hour qualification test durations raises questions about whether current pass/fail criteria capture long-term degradation behavior [REF:13][REF:16].\n\n## Design Implications and Research Gaps\n\nEncapsulant selection for damp-heat resistance benefits from comparative adhesion stability data across polymer families. POE coupons demonstrated the best adhesion stability, followed by EPE composites and then EVA [REF:5]. FTIR-ATR analysis showed insignificant bulk degradation in these systems, confirming the critical role of UV and moisture in accelerating encapsulant degradation at interfaces rather than in the polymer bulk [REF:5]. The POE > EPE > EVA stability ranking [REF:5], combined with the threshold debonding energy framework proposing 160 J/m² for encapsulant interfaces and 10 J/m² for backsheet interfaces to avoid delamination [REF:10], provides a quantitative basis for material selection decisions.\n\n| Encapsulant | Adhesion Stability Ranking | Debonding Energy Threshold | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| POE | Best | 160 J/m² (encapsulant interface) | [REF:5][REF:10] |\n| EPE | Intermediate | 160 J/m² (encapsulant interface) | [REF:5][REF:10] |\n| EVA | Lowest | 160 J/m² (encapsulant interface) | [REF:5][REF:10] |\n| Backsheet interface | — | 10 J/m² | [REF:10] |\n\nMoisture barrier design at the module edge represents a complementary strategy to encapsulant chemistry optimization. Butyl rubber edge seal achieves two orders of magnitude lower Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) compared with UV-curable epoxies and polymeric encapsulants [REF:12]. A bond width of 1.25 cm for butyl rubber with added desiccant was modeled to be adequate for a 25-year lifetime against moisture ingress [REF:12]. The dramatic WVTR differential between butyl rubber and alternative sealants [REF:12] positions edge-seal engineering as a high-leverage intervention point: reducing moisture flux at the module perimeter directly limits the interfacial moisture concentration that kinetic models identify as the primary degradation driver.\n\nProcessing conditions prior to bonding also modulate adhesive performance. Adhesive effectiveness may increase if preheated at 36°C or above before use rather than applied at refrigerator or room temperature [REF:17]. This temperature sensitivity at the application stage implies that thermal conditioning protocols during manufacturing could influence the initial bond quality that subsequently determines damp-heat durability. Separately, dentin bond strengths of adhesive systems decreased with increasing relative humidity across six tested conditions [REF:18]. The humidity sensitivity during bond formation [REF:18] and the preheating benefit [REF:17] together highlight that the environmental conditions at the moment of encapsulant application—not only during subsequent service life—affect long-term adhesion performance.\n\nThe convergence of encapsulant stability rankings [REF:5], debonding energy thresholds [REF:10], and edge-seal moisture barriers [REF:12] defines a multi-layer design framework for damp-heat resistance. Selecting a POE-based encapsulant provides the highest adhesion stability [REF:5], ensuring that the encapsulant interface remains above the 160 J/m² delamination threshold [REF:10] for longer exposure durations. Pairing this with a butyl rubber edge seal at 1.25 cm bond width [REF:12] addresses the moisture ingress pathway from the module perimeter. Process-level controls—including preheating adhesives above 36°C [REF:17] and managing ambient humidity during lamination [REF:18]—add a third layer of protection by maximizing initial bond quality.\n\nSeveral critical research gaps limit the translation of these findings into comprehensive design guidelines for flexible electronics. DVS-PEI-specific aging data are absent from the available evidence base; the stability ranking of POE > EPE > EVA [REF:5] does not include PEI or DVS-modified formulations, leaving their damp-heat adhesion behavior uncharacterized. The debonding energy thresholds of 160 J/m² and 10 J/m² [REF:10] were proposed for specific interface configurations and have not been validated across the range of flexible substrates (polyimide, PET, PEN) used in flexible electronics. Combined stress testing—where damp heat, UV, and mechanical flexing act simultaneously—has not been systematically investigated, despite FTIR evidence that UV and moisture together are the critical accelerants [REF:5]. Long-duration field validation correlating the butyl rubber edge-seal model predictions [REF:12] with actual 25-year moisture ingress performance remains outstanding. The humidity and temperature sensitivity of initial bond formation [REF:17][REF:18] has not been quantified for encapsulant lamination processes specifically, creating uncertainty about how manufacturing environment control translates to field durability.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- POE demonstrates the best adhesion stability, followed by EPE and then EVA, with FTIR confirming that interface degradation rather than bulk polymer degradation drives failure under UV and moisture exposure [REF:5].\n- Threshold debonding energies of 160 J/m² for encapsulant interfaces and 10 J/m² for backsheet interfaces define quantitative delamination avoidance targets [REF:10].\n- Butyl rubber edge seal provides two orders of magnitude lower WVTR than UV-curable epoxies, with a modeled 1.25 cm bond width adequate for 25-year moisture protection [REF:12].\n- Adhesive effectiveness increases with preheating above 36°C [REF:17], and bond strength decreases with increasing relative humidity during application [REF:18], indicating manufacturing environment as a durability variable.\n- DVS-PEI-specific damp-heat aging data, combined stress testing, and long-duration field validation of edge-seal performance represent the most pressing research gaps for flexible electronics encapsulation.\n\n## References\n\n[1] Liu K, Miller D, Bosco N (2025).
