The Impact of the Lamination Process on the Adhesion Properties at the Glass-Encapsulant Interface a. https://publica.fraunhofer.de/bitstreams/60c176eb-f411-427a-afed-276f657faf88/download\n",
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4111:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:5330:  "final_report": "# How does damp heat aging affect the adhesion strength of DVS-PEI encapsulants in flexible electronics?\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis systematic review synthesizes evidence from 19 sources examining how damp heat aging affects encapsulant adhesion strength in flexible electronics, with particular attention to the uncharacterized DVS-PEI material class. Under combined thermal-humid exposure at 95°C, adhesive joint tensile strength decreased by 54.4%, nearly double the 31.3% reduction from moisture absorption alone [6], following Arrhenius kinetics proportional to interfacial moisture concentration [3]. Thermal cycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) produced no statistically significant effect on interfacial mechanics (p > 0.05) [4], confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver. EVA at the glass interface degrades preferentially, reaching levels equivalent to 15+ years of field exposure after 10,000 hours of accelerated aging [13]. Industry data reveal that only 69% of module configurations pass damp heat qualification [16], with 15 manufacturers experiencing failures. Formulation optimization identifies 24% VA content in PEVA [15] and 84–90% gel content in EVA [10] as durability optima, while POE demonstrates superior adhesion stability over EVA [5]. A threshold debonding energy of 160 J/m² is proposed for encapsulant interfaces [10]. The most critical gap identified is the near-complete absence of DVS-PEI-specific damp heat aging data across all dimensions—kinetics, failure modes, formulation sensitivity, and field correlation.\n\n## Encapsulant Materials and Damp Heat Test Standards\n\nInterface degradation and bulk encapsulant integrity do not necessarily track together under environmental stress. Thermal degradation at the encapsulant-substrate interface can reach significant levels even when the bulk encapsulant material itself shows negligible degradation in the absence of UV radiation and elevated humidity [REF:1]. This decoupling between interfacial and bulk failure modes underscores the need for adhesion-specific characterization rather than reliance on bulk polymer metrics alone.\n\nAmong encapsulant systems evaluated under accelerated aging conditions, cross-linking encapsulants such as ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA) and polyolefin elastomer (POE) demonstrate sensitivity to processing parameters. Higher lamination temperature proved beneficial for both peel force and gel content, with improvements observed before and after damp-heat aging [REF:2]. Adhesion between encapsulant and glass was characterized through peel tests, while gel content and yellowness index served as complementary aging indicators [REF:2]. The co-measurement of mechanical adhesion alongside chemical aging markers enables a more complete picture of encapsulant performance under stress [REF:2].\n\nThe observation that lamination temperature influences post-aging adhesion retention [REF:2] while interface degradation can proceed independently of bulk degradation [REF:1] points to a complex failure landscape. Process optimization at the lamination stage may mitigate adhesion loss, yet the interfacial degradation pathway operates through mechanisms not fully captured by bulk indicators. Current evidence is limited to EVA and POE systems; the adhesion behavior of other encapsulant families under equivalent damp-heat protocols remains an open question requiring dedicated investigation.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Thermal degradation at the encapsulant-substrate interface can be significant even when the bulk encapsulant experiences negligible degradation without UV and humidity exposure [REF:1].\n- Higher lamination temperature improves both peel force and gel content for cross-linking encapsulants (EVA, POE) before and after damp-heat aging [REF:2].\n- Peel tests, gel content, and yellowness index together provide a multi-indicator assessment of adhesion and encapsulant aging [REF:2].\n- Evidence is currently limited to EVA and POE; other encapsulant families lack equivalent damp-heat adhesion characterization.\n\n## Adhesion Degradation Kinetics Under Damp Heat\n\nAdhesion energy between cell, encapsulant, and glass layers decreases under both hot-dry and hot-humid accelerated aging conditions. Coupon-level testing after 5000 h of exposure at 90°C under ~1% RH (hot-dry) and 60% RH (hot-humid) demonstrated measurable decreases in adhesion energy Gc [REF:5]. The presence of moisture amplifies the degradation pathway: tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by approximately 31.3% after moisture absorption alone, while combined exposure to high temperature (95°C) and humidity drove losses to 54.4% [REF:6]. These quantitative results converge with kinetic modeling that describes the adhesion degradation rate as proportional to the moisture concentration at the interface, governed by an Arrhenius temperature dependence [REF:3]. Higher temperature increases both the rate of chemical reaction and the diffusion coefficient, promoting greater water penetration and further degradation of adhesive joints [REF:6].\n\nThe mechanistic picture emerging from these data distinguishes between reversible and irreversible damage modes. Interface strength reduction, the appearance of microstructure defects, and hydrolysis of the polymer matrix are all irreversible consequences of damp-heat aging [REF:6]. This irreversibility contrasts with the behavior observed under purely thermal cycling. Thermocycling over 10,000 cycles between 5°C and 50°C had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on the interfacial mechanics and fracture mode of either experimental or commercial adhesives [REF:4]. The divergence between moisture-driven and thermally driven outcomes reinforces the kinetic model's emphasis on moisture concentration as the rate-controlling variable at the interface [REF:3][REF:4].\n\n| Aging Condition | Degradation Observed | Source |\n|---|---|---|\n| Hot-dry, 90°C, ~1% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |\n| Hot-humid, 90°C, 60% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |\n| Moisture absorption only | Tensile strength −31.3% | [REF:6] |\n| Combined 95°C + humidity | Tensile strength −54.4% | [REF:6] |\n| Thermocycling, 10,000 cycles, 5–50°C | No significant effect (p > 0.05) | [REF:4] |\n\nComparative performance across encapsulant chemistries reveals material-dependent degradation rates under damp heat. EVA laminates showed less degradation in peel force after damp heat than POE samples across the tested lamination temperatures [REF:2]. POE laminates, by contrast, revealed improved compressive shear performance after damp-heat aging compared to their unaged state [REF:7]. This apparent divergence—EVA outperforming POE in peel force retention while POE improves in shear—indicates that adhesion degradation kinetics are both material-specific and test-mode dependent [REF:2][REF:7]. Peel strength and shear strength capture different interfacial failure mechanisms, and ranking encapsulants by one metric alone risks mischaracterizing their damp-heat durability.\n\nThe kinetic framework linking degradation rate to interfacial moisture concentration and Arrhenius-type temperature dependence [REF:3] provides a unifying model, yet its applicability across all encapsulant families remains unverified. Current quantitative data concentrate on EVA and POE systems [REF:2][REF:7], with adhesion energy measurements on cell/encapsulant/glass configurations [REF:5] and tensile testing on adhesive joints exposed to combined thermo-humid conditions [REF:6]. The absence of equivalent fracture energy (kJ/m²) measurements for TPO or other polyolefin encapsulants under standardized damp-heat protocols represents a significant evidence gap. Similarly, the irreversible nature of hydrolysis-driven degradation [REF:6] raises durability questions that Arrhenius extrapolation from accelerated tests may not fully capture, particularly when the dominant failure mechanism transitions from interfacial debonding to bulk polymer degradation at longer exposure durations.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by 31.3% under moisture absorption alone and by 54.4% under combined high temperature (95°C) and humidity, confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver [REF:6].\n- Adhesion energy Gc decreased after 5000 h of both hot-dry (90°C, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90°C, 60% RH) aging on cell/encapsulant/glass coupons [REF:5].\n- Thermocycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on interfacial mechanics, isolating moisture as the critical variable [REF:4].\n- EVA laminates retained peel force better than POE under damp heat [REF:2], while POE showed improved compressive shear after aging [REF:7], indicating test-mode-dependent degradation ranking.\n- Interface strength reduction, microstructure defects, and polymer matrix hydrolysis are irreversible effects of damp-heat exposure [REF:6].\n\n## Failure Mode Transitions and Interfacial Mechanisms\n\nSubstrate type and aging method exert statistically significant effects on bond strength, while the choice of adhesive approach does not. Three-way ANOVA demonstrated that substrate (p = 0.00) and aging method (p = 0.00) were significant factors, whereas adhesive approach had no significant influence (p = 0.84) [REF:8]. This finding reframes the failure mode question: degradation outcomes depend more on the interface being stressed and the environmental protocol applied than on the adhesive formulation itself [REF:8]. The resistance of resin-dentin bonds to thermal-aging degradation was material dependent [REF:11], and among the adhesive systems tested, one-step self-etch adhesives obtained the lowest microtensile bond strength (MTBS) results after both aging treatments, with significantly reduced adhesive capacity following thermocycling [REF:11]. Not all adhesive chemistries, however, succumb to aging: the experimental non-methacrylate adhesive EA 5 experienced no changes (p > 0.05) in bonding performance following the entire 1-year duration of biochemical aging [REF:4].\n\n| Factor | p-value | Significance | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Substrate | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |\n| Aging method | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |\n| Adhesive approach | 0.84 | Not significant | [REF:8] |\n\nThe chemical degradation pathway in EVA encapsulants under damp heat proceeds through decomposition into acetic acid, which in turn reacts with perovskite layers and causes yellow PbI2 degradation [REF:12]. This acetic acid release represents a failure mechanism specific to EVA chemistry and distinguishes it from non-acetate-containing encapsulants. At the interfacial level, EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, explaining why this initially stronger interface becomes the preferential site for delamination [REF:14]. The differential degradation across the two EVA interfaces [REF:14] aligns with the ANOVA finding that substrate type drives bond strength outcomes [REF:8], as the glass and cell surfaces present fundamentally different chemical environments for moisture interaction and adhesive breakdown.\n\nMaterial properties beyond chemistry further constrain encapsulant durability. PEVA18 showed inadequate transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of damp-heat aging, discouraging its use as an encapsulant [REF:9]. Elevated stiffness after aging increases the mechanical stress at the interface during thermal cycling, potentially lowering the delamination threshold. This stiffness-mediated pathway operates alongside the chemical degradation routes documented for EVA [REF:12][REF:14] and represents an independent contributor to failure mode transitions in polyethylene-vinyl acetate variants [REF:9].\n\nThe long-term trajectory of adhesion loss provides a bridge between accelerated laboratory aging and field performance. At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion falls to a level generally seen after long-term field exposures of more than 15 years [REF:13]. The EVA manufacturer STR has suggested a minimum of 53 N/cm peel strength after lamination (measured by 180° peel) as sufficient to ensure a 20-year lifetime [REF:10]. These two data points—the 10,000-hour laboratory equivalence to >15 years of field exposure [REF:13] and the 53 N/cm durability threshold [REF:10]—together frame the practical boundary conditions. When accelerated aging drives peel strength below this manufacturer-specified floor, the encapsulant system transitions from a regime of gradual interfacial weakening to one where delamination becomes the dominant failure mode.\n\nSeveral methodological limitations constrain these findings. The statistical independence of adhesive approach from bond strength [REF:8] was demonstrated in a specific substrate-adhesive matrix and may not generalize to all encapsulant-substrate combinations encountered in flexible electronics. The EA 5 result showing zero degradation over one year of biochemical aging [REF:4] applies to a non-methacrylate formulation that differs fundamentally from the EVA and POE systems central to photovoltaic encapsulation. The EVA deacetylation pathway producing acetic acid [REF:12] is chemistry-specific and does not extend to polyolefin or thermoplastic encapsulants. Quantitative fracture energy measurements that would enable direct comparison of failure mode transitions across encapsulant families remain sparse in the available evidence.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Substrate type and aging method significantly affect bond strength (p = 0.00 for both), while adhesive approach does not (p = 0.84), indicating that interfacial conditions dominate over adhesive formulation [REF:8].\n- EVA decomposes into acetic acid under damp heat, reacting with perovskite and causing PbI2 degradation, a failure mechanism specific to acetate-containing encapsulants [REF:12].\n- EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, making the initially stronger glass interface the preferential delamination site [REF:14].\n- At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion reaches levels equivalent to >15 years of field exposure, against a manufacturer-recommended minimum of 53 N/cm for 20-year lifetime [REF:13][REF:10].\n- The experimental non-methacrylate adhesive EA 5 showed no degradation (p > 0.05) over 1 year of biochemical aging, suggesting alternative chemistries may resist aging-induced failure modes [REF:4].\n\n## Formulation and Processing Parameters Affecting Durability\n\nVinyl acetate (VA) content exerts a decisive influence on PEVA encapsulant durability under damp-heat conditions. PEVA films with 18, 24, 33, and 40% VA content were cured at 150°C under vacuum and subjected to damp-heat aging at 85°C and 85% RH per IEC 61215 for 1000 and 2000 h [REF:9][REF:15]. PEVA with higher VA content (33% and 40%) degraded significantly after 2000 hours of damp-heat aging, resulting in significant loss of transmittance and mechanical integrity [REF:15]. Tensile strength, thermal stability, and degree of crosslinking were greater for PEVA containing lower VA content (18% and 24%) and diminished for higher VA (33% and 40%) after damp-heat aging [REF:15]. The pattern is consistent across multiple performance axes: lower VA content preserves both mechanical and thermal properties more effectively under prolonged moisture-heat exposure.\n\n| VA Content | Tensile Strength After DH | Thermal Stability After DH | Crosslinking After DH | Encapsulant Recommendation | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 18% (PEVA18) | Higher | Higher | Higher | Discouraged (stiffness, transmittance) | [REF:15][REF:9] |\n| 24% | Higher | Higher | Higher | Recommended | [REF:9][REF:15] |\n| 33% | Diminished | Diminished | Diminished | Not recommended | [REF:15] |\n| 40% | Diminished | Diminished | Diminished | Not recommended | [REF:15] |\n\nThe lowest VA formulation, however, does not represent the optimal choice despite its superior mechanical retention. PEVA18 (18% VA) showed inadequate percent transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of damp-heat aging, discouraging its use as an encapsulant [REF:15]. This creates a formulation window: while higher VA content (33–40%) compromises durability and lower VA content (18%) compromises optical and mechanical functionality, the intermediate 24% VA formulation balances these competing requirements. Both independent investigations converge on the same recommendation—reducing VA content in PEVA to 24% for better encapsulation response against damp-heat aging [REF:9][REF:15].\n\nCrosslink density, quantified through gel content, provides a complementary parameter governing damp-heat resistance. The optimal gel content range for the EVA encapsulant materials tested was 84–90%, where laminates showed the best combined chemical, optical, and adhesion stability [REF:10]. Samples below 70% gel content showed low chemical and optical stability and weak adhesion [REF:10]. Gel content thus serves as a process-quality indicator: encapsulants laminated below the 70% threshold enter service with insufficient crosslink density to withstand hygrothermal stress, while those within the 84–90% range retain interfacial integrity through the aging protocol.\n\nThe interplay between VA content and crosslinking density defines a two-dimensional formulation space for damp-heat durability. Lower VA content (18–24%) preserves crosslinking capacity after aging [REF:15], and higher gel content (84–90%) ensures adhesion retention [REF:10]. The PEVA18 case illustrates that maximizing one parameter without considering the other leads to suboptimal outcomes: despite superior crosslinking retention at low VA, the 18% formulation fails on transmittance and stiffness criteria [REF:15]. The 24% VA target [REF:9][REF:15] combined with the 84–90% gel content window [REF:10] together define the processing envelope most likely to deliver long-term encapsulant performance.\n\nSeveral gaps limit the generalizability of these formulation guidelines. The VA content studies examined PEVA specifically [REF:9][REF:15], and the gel content optimization was conducted on a particular EVA material [REF:10]; whether the 24% VA and 84–90% gel content optima transfer to other polyolefin or thermoplastic encapsulant families remains uncharacterized. The aging protocol followed IEC 61215 at 85°C/85% RH [REF:9], a standardized but singular condition that does not capture the full range of field exposure profiles, including cyclic humidity or UV co-exposure. Dose-response data between VA content and specific degradation products (such as acetic acid yield) are absent from the available claims, limiting mechanistic interpretation of why the 24–33% VA boundary marks the transition from acceptable to unacceptable degradation.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- PEVA with higher VA content (33% and 40%) degraded significantly after 2000 h of damp-heat aging (85°C/85% RH), with loss of transmittance, tensile strength, thermal stability, and crosslinking [REF:15].\n- PEVA18 (18% VA) retained superior mechanical properties but showed inadequate transmittance and high stiffness after aging, ruling it out as an encapsulant [REF:15].\n- Two independent sources recommend reducing VA content to 24% for optimal damp-heat resistance [REF:9][REF:15].\n- The optimal gel content range for EVA is 84–90%, with samples below 70% showing low chemical/optical stability and weak adhesion [REF:10].\n- The intersection of 24% VA content and 84–90% gel content defines the formulation window most protective against damp-heat degradation [REF:9][REF:10][REF:15].\n\n## Field Reliability and Industry Quality Trends\n\nIndustry-scale reliability testing reveals that damp-heat performance remains a significant quality challenge across the photovoltaic module supply chain. In the 2024 PVEL scorecard, only 69% of bills of materials (BOMs) tested degraded by less than 6% power loss after damp-heat testing [REF:16]. Fifteen manufacturers experienced at least one failure during damp-heat testing in the 2024 reporting period [REF:16]. The involvement of multiple manufacturers in these failure events indicates that the problem is distributed across the supply base rather than concentrated in isolated producers.\n\n| Metric | Value | Source |\n|---|---|---|\n| BOMs with <6% power loss after DH | 69% | [REF:16] |\n| Manufacturers with at least one DH failure | 15 | [REF:16] |\n\nThe temporal dynamics of adhesion degradation under damp-heat conditions follow a non-linear trajectory that complicates durability predictions. Both field exposure and accelerated chamber aging reveal a distinct adhesion plateau that emerges during intermediate exposure periods—after 1 year in the field and after 1,000 hours in a chamber [REF:13]. This plateau behavior means that short-duration accelerated tests may overestimate the rate of long-term degradation by extrapolating from the initial steep decline phase, while the plateau phase itself does not guarantee permanent stabilization at acceptable adhesion levels.\n\nA parallel non-monotonic pattern has been observed in single lap joint strength under damp-heat aging: the strength first decreased and then rose with aging time, a trend attributed to post-curing of the adhesive making it harder [REF:6]. This recovery phenomenon contrasts with the monotonic degradation typically assumed in accelerated aging models and has implications for interpreting short-duration test results. The adhesion plateau observed in field and chamber studies [REF:13] and the post-cure-driven strength recovery in lap joints [REF:6] together indicate that adhesion evolution under damp heat is not a simple monotonic decline—transient recovery or stabilization phases intervene, potentially masking underlying progressive degradation.\n\n| Aging Stage | Adhesion Trend | Mechanism | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Initial (<1 year field / <1000 h chamber) | Declining | Moisture ingress, interfacial degradation | [REF:13] |\n| Intermediate plateau | Stabilized | Plateau formation | [REF:13] |\n| Non-monotonic (lap joints) | Decrease then rise | Post-curing hardening | [REF:6] |\n\nThe 69% pass rate in industry testing [REF:16] must be interpreted against the non-linear adhesion trajectories documented in controlled studies [REF:6][REF:13]. The plateau observed after 1,000 hours of chamber aging [REF:13] aligns with standard qualification test durations (IEC 61215 specifies 1,000 h), raising the possibility that modules passing the 1,000-hour checkpoint benefit from plateau-phase stabilization without being tested through the subsequent degradation phase that may follow at longer exposures. The post-curing recovery in lap joints [REF:6] further complicates pass/fail interpretation: a module that appears to have stabilized may simply be in a transient hardening phase.\n\nThe available evidence base for field reliability assessment carries several methodological limitations. The PVEL scorecard data [REF:16] report power degradation thresholds rather than direct adhesion measurements, requiring inference about the relationship between power loss and encapsulant adhesion failure. The adhesion plateau observation [REF:13] was derived from specific module configurations and aging protocols, and its universality across encapsulant chemistries and substrate combinations has not been established. The non-monotonic strength trend in single lap joints [REF:6] involves a post-curing mechanism that is adhesive-specific and may not apply to all encapsulant systems. Longer-duration accelerated aging studies exceeding the plateau phase, combined with direct adhesion measurements at the module level, would strengthen the link between laboratory findings and field failure prediction.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Only 69% of BOMs tested in the 2024 PVEL scorecard degraded by less than 6% power loss after damp-heat testing, with 15 manufacturers experiencing at least one failure [REF:16].\n- A distinct adhesion plateau emerges after 1 year of field exposure and 1,000 hours of chamber aging, indicating non-linear degradation kinetics [REF:13].\n- Single lap joint strength follows a non-monotonic trajectory under damp-heat aging—first decreasing, then rising due to post-curing of the adhesive [REF:6].\n- The coincidence of the adhesion plateau with standard 1,000-hour qualification test durations raises questions about whether current pass/fail criteria capture long-term degradation behavior [REF:13][REF:16].\n\n## Design Implications and Research Gaps\n\nEncapsulant selection for damp-heat resistance benefits from comparative adhesion stability data across polymer families. POE coupons demonstrated the best adhesion stability, followed by EPE composites and then EVA [REF:5]. FTIR-ATR analysis showed insignificant bulk degradation in these systems, confirming the critical role of UV and moisture in accelerating encapsulant degradation at interfaces rather than in the polymer bulk [REF:5]. The POE > EPE > EVA stability ranking [REF:5], combined with the threshold debonding energy framework proposing 160 J/m² for encapsulant interfaces and 10 J/m² for backsheet interfaces to avoid delamination [REF:10], provides a quantitative basis for material selection decisions.\n\n| Encapsulant | Adhesion Stability Ranking | Debonding Energy Threshold | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| POE | Best | 160 J/m² (encapsulant interface) | [REF:5][REF:10] |\n| EPE | Intermediate | 160 J/m² (encapsulant interface) | [REF:5][REF:10] |\n| EVA | Lowest | 160 J/m² (encapsulant interface) | [REF:5][REF:10] |\n| Backsheet interface | — | 10 J/m² | [REF:10] |\n\nMoisture barrier design at the module edge represents a complementary strategy to encapsulant chemistry optimization. Butyl rubber edge seal achieves two orders of magnitude lower Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) compared with UV-curable epoxies and polymeric encapsulants [REF:12]. A bond width of 1.25 cm for butyl rubber with added desiccant was modeled to be adequate for a 25-year lifetime against moisture ingress [REF:12]. The dramatic WVTR differential between butyl rubber and alternative sealants [REF:12] positions edge-seal engineering as a high-leverage intervention point: reducing moisture flux at the module perimeter directly limits the interfacial moisture concentration that kinetic models identify as the primary degradation driver.\n\nProcessing conditions prior to bonding also modulate adhesive performance. Adhesive effectiveness may increase if preheated at 36°C or above before use rather than applied at refrigerator or room temperature [REF:17]. This temperature sensitivity at the application stage implies that thermal conditioning protocols during manufacturing could influence the initial bond quality that subsequently determines damp-heat durability. Separately, dentin bond strengths of adhesive systems decreased with increasing relative humidity across six tested conditions [REF:18]. The humidity sensitivity during bond formation [REF:18] and the preheating benefit [REF:17] together highlight that the environmental conditions at the moment of encapsulant application—not only during subsequent service life—affect long-term adhesion performance.\n\nThe convergence of encapsulant stability rankings [REF:5], debonding energy thresholds [REF:10], and edge-seal moisture barriers [REF:12] defines a multi-layer design framework for damp-heat resistance. Selecting a POE-based encapsulant provides the highest adhesion stability [REF:5], ensuring that the encapsulant interface remains above the 160 J/m² delamination threshold [REF:10] for longer exposure durations. Pairing this with a butyl rubber edge seal at 1.25 cm bond width [REF:12] addresses the moisture ingress pathway from the module perimeter. Process-level controls—including preheating adhesives above 36°C [REF:17] and managing ambient humidity during lamination [REF:18]—add a third layer of protection by maximizing initial bond quality.\n\nSeveral critical research gaps limit the translation of these findings into comprehensive design guidelines for flexible electronics. DVS-PEI-specific aging data are absent from the available evidence base; the stability ranking of POE > EPE > EVA [REF:5] does not include PEI or DVS-modified formulations, leaving their damp-heat adhesion behavior uncharacterized. The debonding energy thresholds of 160 J/m² and 10 J/m² [REF:10] were proposed for specific interface configurations and have not been validated across the range of flexible substrates (polyimide, PET, PEN) used in flexible electronics. Combined stress testing—where damp heat, UV, and mechanical flexing act simultaneously—has not been systematically investigated, despite FTIR evidence that UV and moisture together are the critical accelerants [REF:5]. Long-duration field validation correlating the butyl rubber edge-seal model predictions [REF:12] with actual 25-year moisture ingress performance remains outstanding. The humidity and temperature sensitivity of initial bond formation [REF:17][REF:18] has not been quantified for encapsulant lamination processes specifically, creating uncertainty about how manufacturing environment control translates to field durability.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- POE demonstrates the best adhesion stability, followed by EPE and then EVA, with FTIR confirming that interface degradation rather than bulk polymer degradation drives failure under UV and moisture exposure [REF:5].\n- Threshold debonding energies of 160 J/m² for encapsulant interfaces and 10 J/m² for backsheet interfaces define quantitative delamination avoidance targets [REF:10].\n- Butyl rubber edge seal provides two orders of magnitude lower WVTR than UV-curable epoxies, with a modeled 1.25 cm bond width adequate for 25-year moisture protection [REF:12].\n- Adhesive effectiveness increases with preheating above 36°C [REF:17], and bond strength decreases with increasing relative humidity during application [REF:18], indicating manufacturing environment as a durability variable.\n- DVS-PEI-specific damp-heat aging data, combined stress testing, and long-duration field validation of edge-seal performance represent the most pressing research gaps for flexible electronics encapsulation.\n\n## References\n\n[1] Liu K, Miller D, Bosco N (2025). Investigating the Crosslinking, Degradation, and Adhesion Behavior of Photovoltaic Encapsulants Unde. .\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4113:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:7314:      "abstract": "Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a common chronic autoimmune disease. Neutrophils release and their extracellular traps (NETs) tend to result in synovial inflammation and cartilage damage. Huangqin Qingre Chubi Capsule (HQC) is an important herbal formulation for RA treatment and has been used for many years. This study applied an interdisciplinary and integrated strategy combining clinical data, integrative bioinformatics, molecular modeling, and both in vitro and in vivo experiments to elucidate the efficacy and potential anti-inflammatory mechanisms of HQC for RA. Retrospective clinical data mining demonstrated that patients with RA treated with HQC exhibited recovery of immuno-inflammatory markers and self-perception. By applying network pharmacological analysis, molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulation, and cellular thermal shift assays, we determined that HQC can directly bind to MAPK14 and target p38 MAPK signaling pathway and NETs formation. Treatment of an adjuvant-induced arthritis rat model combining damp-heat patterns using HQC demonstrated it's effectiveness in reducing the severity of inflammatory arthritis in a dose-dependent manner and downregulated phosphorylation of p38 MAPK and NETs release. In vitro co-culture system mimicking inflammatory microenvironment of RA in vivo revealed that crosstalk between neutrophils (PMNs) and fibroblast-like synoviocytes (FLSs) was manifested by activation of p38 MAPK signaling pathway, release of NETs, and amplification of inflammation, which was blocked by HQC. Mechanistic validation using the p38 MAPK agonist diprovocim demonstrated that HQC inhibited NET formation by modulating the p38 MAPK pathway (mainly by inhibiting its phosphorylation) to exert anti-inflammatory effects. In conclusion, our interdisciplinary and integrated study demonstrated that HQC can target the p38 MAPK signaling pathway to inhibit NET formation and inflammatory response, thereby blocking the crosstalk between PMNs and FLSs to ameliorating RA.",

.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_14\codex_stderr.log:1687:outputs\polaris_graph\WEB_20260312T033032_01f8ec.json:1950:      "content": "crosslinked polyethylene xlpe: Topics by Science.gov Toggle navigation Topics by Science.gov Home About Science.gov Sample records for crosslinked polyethylene xlpe &laquo; 1 2 3 4 5 &raquo; Wear performance of neat and vitamin E blended highly cross-linked PE under severe conditions: The combined effect of accelerated ageing and third body particles during wear test. PubMed Affatato, Saverio; De Mattia, Jonathan Salvatore; Bracco, Pierangiola; Pavoni, Eleonora; Taddei, Paola 2016-12-01 The objective of this study is to evaluate the effects of third-body particles on the in vitro wear behaviour of three different sets of polyethylene acetabular cups after prolonged testing in a hip simulator and accelerated ageing. Vitamin E-blended, cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE_VE), cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) and conventional polyethylene (STD_PE) acetabular cups were simulator tested for two million cycles under severe conditions (i.e. by adding third-body particles to the bovine calf serum lubricant). Micro-Fourier Transform Infrared and micro-Raman spectroscopic analyses, differential scanning calorimetry, and crosslink density measurements were used to characterize the samples at a molecular level. The STD_PE cups had twice mass loss than the XLPE_VE components and four times than the XLPE samples; statistically significant differences were found between the mass losses of the three sets of cups. The observed wear trend was justified on the basis of the differences in cross-link density among the samples (XLPE>XLPE_VE>STD_PE). FTIR crystallinity profiles, bulk DSC crystallinity and surface micro-Raman crystallinity seemed to have a similar behaviour upon testing: all of them (as well as the all-trans and ortho-trans contents) revealed the most significant changes in XLPE and XLPE_VE samples. The more severe third-body wear testing conditions determined more noticeable changes in all spectroscopic markers with respect to previous tests. Unexpectedly, traces of bulk oxidation were found in both STD_PE (unirradiated) and XLPE (remelting-stabilized), which were expected to be stable to oxidation; on the contrary, XLPE_VE demonstrated a high oxidative stability in the present, highly demanding conditions. Towards aging mechanisms of cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) cable insulation materials in nuclear power plants DOE Office of Scientific and Technical Information (OSTI.GOV) Liu, Shuaishuai; Fifield, Leonard S.; Bowler, Nicola Cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) cable insulation material undergoes simultaneous, accelerated thermal and gamma-radiation aging to simulate the long-term aging environment within nuclear power plants (NPPs). A variety of materials characterization tests, including scanning electron microscopy, thermo-gravimetric analysis, differential scanning calorimetry, oxidation induction time, gel-fraction and dielectric properties measurement, are conducted on pristine and differently aged XLPE samples. A preliminary model of one possible aging mechanism of XLPE cable insulation material under gamma radiation at elevated temperature of 115 Â°C is suggested. Does cyclic stress and accelerated ageing influence the wear behavior of highly crosslinked polyethylene? PubMed Affatato, Saverio; De Mattia, Jonathan Salvatore; Bracco, Pierangiola; Pavoni, Eleonora; Taddei, Paola 2016-06-01 First-generation (irradiated and remelted or annealed) and second-generation (irradiated and vitamin E blended or doped) highly crosslinked polyethylenes were introduced in the last decade to solve the problems of wear and osteolysis. In this study, the influence of the Vitamin-E addition on crosslinked polyethylene (XLPE_VE) was evaluated by comparing the in vitro wear behavior of crosslinked polyethylene (XLPE) versus Vitamin-E blended polyethylene XLPE and conventional ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene (STD_PE) acetabular cups, after accelerated ageing according to ASTM F2003-02 (70.0Â±0.1Â°C, pure oxygen at 5bar for 14 days). The test was performed using a hip joint simulator run for two millions cycles, under bovine calf serum as lubricant. Mass loss was found to decrease along the series XLPE_VE>STD_PE>XLPE, although no statistically significant differences were found between the mass losses of the three sets of cups. Micro-Raman spectroscopy was used to investigate at a molecular level the morphology changes induced by wear. The spectroscopic analyses showed that the accelerated ageing determined different wear mechanisms and molecular rearrangements during testing with regards to the changes in both the chain orientation and the distribution of the all-trans sequences within the orthorhombic, amorphous and third phases. The results of the present study showed that the addition of vitamin E was not effective to improve the gravimetric wear of PE after accelerated ageing. However, from a molecular point of view, the XLPE_VE acetabular cups tested after accelerated ageing appeared definitely less damaged than the STD_PE ones and comparable to XLPE samples. Copyright Â© 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Three Year RSA Evaluation of Vitamin E Diffused Highly Cross-linked Polyethylene Liners and Cup Stability. PubMed Sillesen, Nanna H; Greene, Meridith E; Nebergall, Audrey K; Nielsen, Poul T; Laursen, Mogens B; Troelsen, Anders; Malchau, Henrik 2015-07-01 Vitamin E diffusion into highly cross-linked polyethylene (E-XLPE) is a method for enhancing oxidative stability of acetabular liners. The purpose of this study was to evaluate in vivo penetration of E-XLPE using radiostereometric analysis (RSA). Eighty-four hips were recruited into a prospective 10-year RSA. This is the first evaluation of the multicenter cohort after 3-years. All patients received E-XLPE liners (E1, Biomet) and porous-titanium coated cups (Regenerex, Biomet). There was no difference (P=0.450) in median femoral head penetration into the E-XLPE liners at 3-years comparing cobalt-chrome heads (-0.028mm; inter-quartile range (IQR) - 0.065 to 0.047) with ceramic heads (-0.043mm, IQR - 0.143to0.042). The 3-year follow-up indicates minimal E-XLPE liner penetration regardless of head material and minimal early cup movement. Copyright Â© 2015 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved. Analysis of simultaneous thermal/gamma radiation aging of cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) insulationâinterim status report DOE Office of Scientific and Technical Information (OSTI.GOV) Fifield, Leonard S.; Correa, Miguel Cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) is the most common cable insulation found in nuclear containment, and is therefore a priority material for investigation of long term aging effect from elevated temperature combined with gamma radiation exposure. Prior work has identified the possibility of anomalous aging behavior in XLPE such as the inverse temperature effect in which radiation exposure is more damaging at lower temperatures than at higher temperatures. We explored simultaneous aging of XLPE insulation from modern FirewallÂ® III nuclear cables at 60, 90, and 115 Â°C, at gamma dose rates from 116 to 540 Gy/h, for exposure periods up to 25moreÂ Â» d. XLPE samples exposed in this way were characterized using the percent gel and uptake factor method. For the conditions and material examine, degradation behavior was seen to track proportionally with increasing temperature, rather than to exhibit greater degradation at lower temperatures. Ongoing work including similar aging at 25 Â°C and characterization of the XLPE samples using other methods will further elucidate these initial resultsÂ«Â less Wear of a 5 megarad cross-linked polyethylene liner: a 6-year RSA study. PubMed Callary, Stuart A; Campbell, David G; Mercer, Graham; Nilsson, Kjell G; Field, John R 2013-07-01 One cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) liner is manufactured using a lower dose of radiation, 5 Mrad, which may result in less cross-linking. The reported in vivo wear rate of this XLPE liner in patients undergoing THA has varied, and has included some patients in each reported cohort who had greater than 0.1 mm/year of wear, which is an historical threshold for osteolysis. Previous studies have measured wear on plain radiographs, an approach that has limited sensitivity. We therefore measured the amount and direction of wear at 6 years using Radiostereometric analysis (RSA) in patients who had THAs that included a cross-linked polyethylene liner manufactured using 5 Mrad radiation. We prospectively reviewed wear in 30 patients who underwent primary THAs with the same design of cross-linked acetabular liner and a 28-mm articulation. Tantalum markers were inserted during surgery and all patients had RSA radiographic examinations at 1 week, 6 months, 1, 2, and 6 years postoperatively. The mean proximal, two-dimensional (2-D) and three-dimensional (3-D) wear rates calculated between 1 year and 6 years were 0.014, 0.014, and 0.018 mm/per year, respectively. The direction of the head penetration recorded between 1 week and 6 years was in a proximal direction for all patients, proximolateral for 16 of 24 patients, and proximomedial for eight of 24 patients. The proximal, 2-D and 3-D wear of a XLPE liner produced using 5 Mrad of radiation was low but measurable by RSA after 6 years. No patients had proximal 2-D or 3-D wear rates exceeding 0.1 mm/year. Further followup is needed to evaluate the effect of XLPE wear particles on the development of long-term osteolysis. The wear of cross-linked polyethylene against itself. **Applies to pipeline A (honest-rebuild). Pipeline B (UI) and pipeline
C (frozen legacy) are governed separately — see `architecture.md`.**

### 9.1 Core Invariants (Non-Negotiable)

1. **Two-family evaluator**: generator and evaluator MUST be from
   different training lineages. `openrouter_client.check_family_segregation`
   raises `RuntimeError` at construction if violated.
2. **Provenance tokens**: every generated sentence carries
   `[#ev:<evidence_id>:<start>-<end>]` tokens. Sentences without valid
   tokens are dropped by `strict_verify`.
3. **Strict verify**: per-sentence check enforces (a) evidence-id in pool,
   (b) span bounds valid, (c) every decimal in sentence appears in span,
   (d) sentence and span share ≥2 content words
   (`PG_PROVENANCE_MIN_CONTENT_OVERLAP`). Fabricated claims fail at least
   one of these.
4. **Zero-verified abort**: if every section fails strict_verify,
   `report.md` is a pipeline-verdict artifact (not an empty-findings
   pseudo-report). Status: `abort_no_verified_sections`.
5. **Corpus approval enforcement**: a corpus with material tier deviation

"""
Regression tests keyed to documented PG_LB_SA_02 content-audit defects.

Each test pins a specific defect from loopback/audit/PG_LB_SA_02_CONTENT_AUDIT.md
so the honest-rebuild work does not silently re-introduce the same pattern.
Tests that cover components not yet built are marked @pytest.mark.xfail with
the phase that will satisfy them.

Plan: C:/Users/msn/.claude/plans/lovely-finding-firefly.md
Phase 1f.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import importlib.util
import json
import sys
import types
from pathlib import Path

import pytest


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Helper: load openrouter_client without its transitive dependencies
# (tracing module requires an async runtime; we mock it for unit tests).
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def _load_openrouter_client():
    """Import openrouter_client with a minimal tracing stub."""
    if "src.polaris_graph.tracing" not in sys.modules:
        pkg_src = types.ModuleType("src")
        pkg_pg = types.ModuleType("src.polaris_graph")
        pkg_tracing = types.ModuleType("src.polaris_graph.tracing")
        pkg_tracing._current_tracer = lambda: None
        sys.modules.setdefault("src", pkg_src)
        sys.modules.setdefault("src.polaris_graph", pkg_pg)
        sys.modules["src.polaris_graph.tracing"] = pkg_tracing
    repo_root = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]
    mod_path = repo_root / "src" / "polaris_graph" / "llm" / "openrouter_client.py"
    spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(
        "polaris_graph_llm_openrouter_for_tests", str(mod_path)
    )
    mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
    spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
    return mod

"""
OpenRouter LLM Client for polaris graph.

Single gateway to Qwen 3.5 Plus via OpenRouter. Two call modes:
- reason(): reasoning ON, returned separately in reasoning_details
- generate(): reasoning OFF, clean prose output only

No CoT scrubbing. No regex filters. No post-processing hacks.
The API handles separation at the protocol level.
"""

import asyncio
import json
import logging
import os
import re
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any, Optional, Type, TypeVar

import httpx
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

from src.polaris_graph.tracing import _current_tracer

load_dotenv()

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

T = TypeVar("T", bound=BaseModel)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Configuration
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

# FIX-305: Persistent cost ledger
_COST_LEDGER_PATH = Path(os.getenv("PG_COST_LEDGER_PATH", "logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl"))

OPENROUTER_API_KEY = os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY", "")
OPENROUTER_BASE_URL = os.getenv(
    "OPENROUTER_BASE_URL", "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"
)
OPENROUTER_MODEL = os.getenv("OPENROUTER_DEFAULT_MODEL", "qwen/qwen3.5-plus-02-15")
OPENROUTER_BUDGET_USD = float(os.getenv("OPENROUTER_BUDGET_USD", "50.0"))

# R-2 (readiness gate): hard per-run cost cap. A single honest-rebuild
# run (scope+retrieval+generate+judge) should cost $0.005-$0.01. Cap at
# $0.10 default so a runaway regen loop or recursive outline can't burn
# hours of budget before we notice. Measured by cumulative cost across
# ALL OpenRouterClient instances instantiated within a single run.
PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN = float(os.getenv("PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN", "0.10"))

# BUG-B-201 fix (pass 2 remediation): per-task ambient state via
# contextvars so concurrent async run_one_query() calls don't stomp
# each other's run_id + cost accumulator.
#
# Pre-fix (commit f632ee5), `_CURRENT_RUN_ID` and `_RUN_COST_USD` were
# module-level globals. Pass 2 showed that under `asyncio.gather`,
# a later run_one_query overwrote the earlier run's ambient id before
# downstream OpenRouterClient() construction — tagging the first run's
# LLM calls with the second run's id.
#
# Post-fix: ContextVar scopes per asyncio Task. Each task sees its own
# run_id + cost without threading explicit kwargs through the call graph.
# Synchronous / serial callers still work because ContextVar defaults
# to the module-default if no set_* has run in the current task.
import contextvars

_RUN_COST_LOCK = __import__("threading").Lock()
_RUN_COST_CTX: contextvars.ContextVar[float] = contextvars.ContextVar(
    "_RUN_COST_USD", default=0.0,
)
_CURRENT_RUN_ID_CTX: contextvars.ContextVar[str | None] = contextvars.ContextVar(
    "_CURRENT_RUN_ID", default=None,
)

| Gate | Threshold | Fail Action |
|---|---|---|
| Corpus adequacy | Min sources per tier (template-driven) | `abort_corpus_inadequate` |
| Corpus approval | Auto-approved OR substantive operator note | `abort_corpus_approval_denied` |
| Strict verify (per sentence) | Numeric match + ≥2 content-word overlap | Drop sentence |
| Strict verify (per section) | ≥40% sentences verified | Attempt one regeneration |
| Strict verify (pipeline) | At least one section with verified prose | `abort_no_verified_sections` |
| Budget | Accumulated cost ≤ `PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN` | `BudgetExceededError` |

### 9.3 Pipeline verdict statuses (manifest.json)

| Status | Condition |
|---|---|
| `success` | All gates passed, generator produced verified prose |
| `abort_scope_rejected` | Scope gate rejected the research question |
| `abort_corpus_inadequate` | Corpus adequacy gate failed (not enough sources) |
| `abort_corpus_approval_denied` | Corpus approval gate rejected (rubber-stamp note on material deviation) |
| `abort_no_verified_sections` | Every generated section failed strict_verify |
| `error_*` | Unexpected failure (API outage, malformed response, etc.) |

### 9.4 Code hygiene (enforced by test suite, not a separate Sheriff)

**Forbidden patterns** (tests in `tests/polaris_graph/` detect these):

- `try: ... except: pass` without logging or re-raise (silent failure)
- `import unittest.mock` or `from unittest.mock import ...` in `src/`
  production code
- Magic numbers (`if score > 0.7` instead of a named constant or env var)
- `time.sleep()` to simulate work
- `# TODO`, `# FIXME`, `# XXX`, `pass` as function body
- Mocking the live-evidence database in integration tests (per user
  feedback memory: integration tests must hit real data sources)

Pipeline C (`src/orchestration/`, `scripts/full_cycle.py`) does not
currently meet these invariants. It is frozen; see
`src/orchestration/FROZEN_SINCE_2026-03-16.md`.

# POLARIS Operational Charter v1

**Authority:** aldrinor only, GPG/SSH-signed commits to polaris-controls
**Effective:** 2026-05-04
**Pinned in POLARIS:** docs/session_pin.txt → charter_sha256
**This document is the highest-authority operational rulebook. Conflicts resolve in favor of this document.**

---

## The 8 Hard Rules

### 1. Roles

- **Claude Code** is architect + reviewer ONLY.
- **Codex CLI** is executor ONLY.
- **User (aldrinor)** is spec owner + merge gate.
- No agent has signing authority on any commit to POLARIS main.
- Out-of-order pipeline execution = blocked by Trusted App server-side.

### 2. Slices

Work proceeds in slices. **One slice active at a time.**

- Slice spec lives at `polaris-controls/slices/<active_slice>.md`
- Claude reads the active slice spec at session start
- Claude does nothing outside its declared scope
- Slice scope = files explicitly listed; everything else is out of bounds

### 3. PR Size Cap

**200 LOC per pull request. Hard ceiling.**

- Trusted App rejects PRs exceeding 200 net additions
- Larger work decomposes into multiple slices, not larger PRs
- No "this PR is special" exceptions

### 4. Golden Tests as Fitness Function

Every slice has golden-master tests in `polaris-controls/golden/<slice_id>/`.

- Tests are immutable to Claude and Codex (admin-only repo, CODEOWNERS-protected)
- Slice is **done** when golden tests pass — then **STOP**
- "Polish," "improve," "while we're at it" beyond passing = forbidden
- Test mutation attempts = HALT condition

### 5. Halt Conditions (mandatory, automatic)

Claude halts and surfaces to user when ANY of:

- Session pin SHA mismatch (any of 4 pinned files)
- Golden test mutation attempted by any agent
- Control-plane file edit attempted (`.github/`, `.codex/`, `polaris-controls/`, `scripts/autoloop/`)
- More than 2 cycles on the same problem
- Codex review unavailable for >1 hour
- PR exceeds 200 LOC
- Reviewer-fatigue indicators trip (>3 PRs queued for human in 24h per project)

### 6. Archive Discipline

Code in `POLARIS/.legacy/` is read-only reference.

- Never imported into production paths (CI gate enforces)
- Never modified post-cutover (CI gate enforces)
- New code lives in canonical paths (`src/`, `web/`, `tests/`, `docs/`, `.codex/`, `scripts/`)
- If old code is needed, it is RE-WRITTEN under the active slice, not copied from `.legacy/`

### 7. Visibility (no private channel exists)

All Claude↔Codex traffic is logged:

- `.codex/loop_log.jsonl` — streaming JSONL, tail-able real-time
- `.codex/slices/<slice_id>/brief.md` — Claude's question to Codex
- `.codex/slices/<slice_id>/verdict.md` — Codex's response
- `.codex/slices/<slice_id>/decision.md` — Claude's decision based on verdict
- `.codex/slices/<slice_id>/diff.patch` — resulting code (if any)

If an exchange is not in these files, it didn't legitimately happen.

### 8. Deviation Resolution

If any document conflicts with this Charter, this Charter wins.
If the Charter cannot be verified against its pinned SHA, Claude HALTS immediately.
Claude does not proceed on memory or interpretation.

---

## Session-Start Ritual (mechanical, every session, every restart)

```
1. Read POLARIS/docs/session_pin.txt
2. Run: gh api repos/aldrinor/polaris-controls/contents/CHARTER.md --jq .sha
   Compare returned blob SHA to pin file's charter_sha256
3. Same for PLAN.md, active slice spec, golden test manifest
4. ANY mismatch → emit halt notice to .codex/halt_<utc>.md; surface to user; do not proceed
5. ALL match → read CHARTER (this doc), PLAN, active slice, golden manifest
6. State to user: active slice ID + current step within slice + next action
7. Wait for user direction OR proceed if direction is unambiguous from slice spec
```

The ritual is enforced because the next PR fails Trusted App verification if any of these were skipped. The ritual is also enforced socially: the user reads the `.codex/` logs and sees whether the ritual ran.

---

## Plan Edit Path (the only legitimate way)

To change Charter, Plan, Slice spec, or Golden tests:

1. User edits the file in polaris-controls
2. User commits with GPG/SSH signature
3. User opens PR in POLARIS updating `docs/session_pin.txt` to the new SHA
4. Trusted App verifies new SHA matches polaris-controls HEAD
5. CI passes; user merges the pin update
6. Next session uses the new pinned versions

Claude never participates in plan edits. If Claude disagrees with a plan, Claude writes `.codex/proposal_<utc>.md` arguing the case and surfaces it. User decides.

---

"""
polaris graph preflight v2 -- comprehensive 40-test validation.

Replaces the old 16-test pg_smoke_test.py with 4 tiers of checks:
  Tier 1 (10): Hard failures -- would crash immediately
  Tier 2 (10): Config range tests -- would crash mid-pipeline
  Tier 3 (15): Integration tests -- would fail during pipeline
  Tier 4 (5):  Quality tests -- would degrade output

Usage:
    python scripts/pg_preflight.py

Set PG_PREFLIGHT_LIVE=1 in .env to run live API tests (Tier 3).
"""

import asyncio
import importlib
import json
import logging
import os
import sys
import tempfile
import time
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

sys.path.insert(0, ".")

from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

logging.basicConfig(
    level=logging.WARNING,
    format="%(asctime)s | %(levelname)-8s | %(message)s",
)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# ANSI colors
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

_GREEN = "\033[92m"
_RED = "\033[91m"
_YELLOW = "\033[93m"
_BOLD = "\033[1m"
_RESET = "\033[0m"

LIVE_MODE = os.getenv("PG_PREFLIGHT_LIVE", "0") == "1"


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Result types
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

PASS = "PASS"
FAIL = "FAIL"
SKIP = "SKIP"


class TestResult:
    """Container for a single test result."""

    def __init__(self, name: str, status: str, message: str):
        self.name = name
        self.status = status
        self.message = message

    def display(self) -> str:
        if self.status == PASS:
            tag = f"{_GREEN}[PASS]{_RESET}"
        elif self.status == FAIL:
            tag = f"{_RED}[FAIL]{_RESET}"
        else:
            tag = f"{_YELLOW}[SKIP]{_RESET}"
        return f"  {tag} {self.name} -- {self.message}"


# ===================================================================
# TIER 1: Hard Failures (10 tests) -- Would crash immediately
# ===================================================================


async def test_openrouter_api_key() -> TestResult:
    """T01: Check OPENROUTER_API_KEY env var exists and is non-empty."""
    key = os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY", "")
    if key and len(key) > 10:
        return TestResult(
            "test_openrouter_api_key",
            PASS,
            f"API key present ({len(key)} chars)",
        )
    return TestResult(
        "test_openrouter_api_key",
        FAIL,
        "OPENROUTER_API_KEY missing or too short",
    )


async def test_serper_api_key() -> TestResult:
    """T02: Check SERPER_API_KEY env var exists."""
    key = os.getenv("SERPER_API_KEY", "")
    if key:
        return TestResult(
            "test_serper_api_key",
            PASS,
            f"API key present ({len(key)} chars)",
        )
    return TestResult(
        "test_serper_api_key",
        FAIL,
        "SERPER_API_KEY missing",
    )


async def test_exa_api_key() -> TestResult:
    """T03: Check EXA_API_KEY exists when PG_EXA_ENABLED=1."""
    exa_enabled = os.getenv("PG_EXA_ENABLED", "1") == "1"
    if not exa_enabled:
        return TestResult(
            "test_exa_api_key",
            SKIP,
            "PG_EXA_ENABLED=0, skipping",
        )
    key = os.getenv("EXA_API_KEY", "")
    if key:
        return TestResult(
            "test_exa_api_key",
            PASS,
            f"API key present ({len(key)} chars)",
        )
    return TestResult(
        "test_exa_api_key",
        FAIL,
        "EXA_API_KEY missing but PG_EXA_ENABLED=1",
    )


async def test_s2_api_key() -> TestResult:
    """T04: Check SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY env var exists."""
    key = os.getenv("SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY", "")
    if key:
        return TestResult(
            "test_s2_api_key",
            PASS,
            f"SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY present ({len(key)} chars)",
        )
    return TestResult(
        "test_s2_api_key",
        FAIL,
        "SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY missing",
    )


async def test_openrouter_budget() -> TestResult:
    """T05: Verify OpenRouter key works and has sufficient budget."""
    key = os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY", "")
    if not key:
        return TestResult(
            "test_openrouter_budget",
            FAIL,
            "No API key to check",
        )
    min_budget = float(os.getenv("PG_BUDGET_GUARD_USD", "5.0"))
    try:
        import httpx

        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30) as client:
            resp = await client.get(
                "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/auth/key",
                headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {key}"},
            )
            if resp.status_code != 200:
                return TestResult(
                    "test_openrouter_budget",
                    FAIL,
                    f"API returned {resp.status_code}: {resp.text[:200]}",
                )
            data = resp.json().get("data", {})
            usage = data.get("usage", 0)
            limit = data.get("limit", None)
            if limit is not None:
                remaining = limit - usage
                if remaining < min_budget:
                    return TestResult(
                        "test_openrouter_budget",
                        FAIL,
                        f"Budget too low: ${remaining:.2f} remaining "
                        f"(minimum: ${min_budget:.2f})",
                    )
                return TestResult(
                    "test_openrouter_budget",
                    PASS,
                    f"${remaining:.2f} remaining (minimum: ${min_budget:.2f})",
                )
            # No limit = unlimited
            return TestResult(
                "test_openrouter_budget",
                PASS,
                f"Unlimited budget (usage: ${usage:.2f})",
            )
    except Exception as exc:
        return TestResult(
            "test_openrouter_budget",
            FAIL,
            f"API call failed: {str(exc)[:200]}",
        )

      invariant tests independent of developer/CI env state, pass `generator_override=""` and
